pick.arc: 'First embodied-AI company' — robotaxi data flywheel + Optimus
pick.note: Framed not as a carmaker but the symbol of the hardware regime shift; explicit 'new pick' 25 Aug 2025 (~$340).

auth.sb: Visser's highest-conviction embodied-AI long: not a carmaker but the first company that can mass-manufacture physical intelligence. New pick 25 August 2025 near $340, up ~45% since.
auth.mb: Visser reframes Tesla entirely – it is the symbol of the regime shift from software AI to hardware AI, the one place embodied intelligence can scale because it already manufactures at scale. Robotaxis are cast as the "ChatGPT moment" of intelligent machines, with FSD's vision-first bet and the Optimus humanoid program as the two engines. The data flywheel is the crux: every car and robot in the field generates vision, motion and outcome data that retrains the models. Traditional auto analysts who model units and margins are, by design, looking at the wrong thing.
auth.lb:
Visser's Tesla call started in March 2025 as a thesis, not a stock pick: AI was moving "from intelligence to embodiment," and Tesla – with its own AI chips, Dojo, battery tech and vertical integration – was the rare name positioned if humanoids and autonomy became real. Through the spring and summer he built the scaffolding around it: the cloud-to-embodied hardware regime shift, the macro setup of a Mag7 that would struggle while the rotation broadened.

The pivot came 25 August 2025, when he made Tesla an explicit "new pick" near $340 in a dedicated paper, "Tesla Robotaxis: The Opening Act of Embodied AI." The argument: robotaxis are the hinge where AI stops being software and becomes the operating system of the physical economy. He asked readers to set aside Musk politics entirely – this is an AI paper, not a stock debate.

What moves it from here is execution on autonomy and Optimus. Removing safety drivers flips the model from carmaker to AI mobility platform; success in vision-based driving feeds straight into humanoid deployment. The flywheel only compounds if the robots ship and the data loops close – that is the bet, and it is up ~45% since the call.
auth.st: Long, high conviction. Anchored ~$278 (call 24 March 2025), explicit new pick 25 August 2025 near $340; current ~$404, return +45.3%. Thesis vehicle for "Embodied AI & Robotics" – robotaxi data flywheel plus Optimus.
auth.mt: The mechanism Visser keeps returning to is the closed flywheel: edge inference on the car/robot generates behavioral, vision and motion data; cloud GPUs train better models on it; models redeploy to the fleet – "rinse, repeat, compound." This is where he argues trillion-dollar GPU capex finally achieves utilization at scale, "not from chatbot queries, but from millions of robots." He frames Optimus against Musk's own "north of $10 trillion in revenue" long-term claim and a $50T global wage pool as the addressable prize for physical labor replacement. The variant view he confronts head-on: the glossy humanoid clips are "illusions of progress" – machines that memorize rather than see, like Waymo's choreographed mapping – so the real test is genuine vision-first generalization, with FSD v14 and the November 6th Optimus showcase as the milestones to watch.
auth.lt:
Across fifteen reports from March 2025 to May 2026, Tesla is the single most-developed name in Visser's coverage and the clearest expression of his core macro thesis: AI is shifting from bits to atoms, cloud to embodiment, and the value is migrating from the LLM concentration (Nvidia/TSMC/memory) out to sensors, batteries, actuators and the firms that can manufacture intelligence into physical bodies. Tesla sits "at the vanguard of its physical expression."

The build is methodical. March 2025 establishes the embodiment arc off Huang's GTC "era of robotics" framing and Musk's Optimus revenue claim. April fleshes out batteries, robotics and Tesla's vertical integration as a picks-and-shovels enabler. August is the inflection: two papers in two days frame Tesla as "the first embodied AI company" and then formalize the long near $340. Through late 2025 and into 2026 he reinforces it with the VLA (vision-language-action) thesis – that kinetic AI needs sub-10ms on-device inference, disqualifying cloud-only architectures – and Musk's own "AI is going to move from thinking to acting" line.

Risks are real and Visser is partly candid about them. His own VLA paper notes Tesla's camera-only approach forces the AI to solve depth computationally and is "brittle in edge cases like nighttime or fog," where LiDAR has a structural advantage – an implicit knock on the vision-first bet he champions. The thesis is also milestone-dependent (robotaxi safety-driver removal, Optimus shipping) and he concedes investors hold "deeply entrenched views" on Musk that cloud the analysis. The flywheel is asserted more than proven.

The honest performance read: the call has worked, +45.3% from the August pick anchor, and Visser's 84% model-portfolio win rate gives the framework credibility. But the return is riding the embodied-AI narrative re-rating, not yet confirmed robotaxi or Optimus economics – this remains a high-conviction bet on a regime shift arriving on schedule, not a proven cash-flow story.

quotes.q: Tesla’s robotaxi network represents the hinge point where AI stops being abstract software and becomes the operating system of the physical economy.
This is going to be a reality in a way that it’s abjectly humiliating to everyone who is an FSD skeptic in the next 12 to 18 months, maybe in the next six months.
At the center of this regime transition is Tesla, which isn’t just an auto company but the first embodied AI company.
AI is going to move from thinking to acting. Real-world AI robots, autonomous vehicles, machines moving through space, this is the true frontier.
Just like AI is difficult for investors or analysts, Tesla is not a car company.
long term, Optimus has the potential to be north of $10 trillion in revenue.

pick.arc: The compute emblem → 'tokens-per-watt' moat → edge/Rubin transition
pick.note: His most-discussed name (26 notes). Conviction wobbled mid-2025 ('power is the new story') but stayed structurally long.

auth.sb: Visser's emblem of the AI buildout and his most-discussed name – up about 74% from his March 2025 call. He stays structurally long, but the story is shifting from training GPUs to inference, edge, and power efficiency.
auth.mb: Nvidia is the anchor of Visser's AI-compute thesis and the stock he writes about most. The core call has held – Nvidia owns the data-center training boom, with Blackwell ramping and the next-gen Vera Rubin platform behind it – but the analysis has matured: the binding constraint is migrating from raw GPU supply to power, memory bandwidth, and getting intelligence onto edge devices. Visser reads Nvidia's absorption of Groq's inference talent as the company itself confirming that the next frontier is efficiency, not just transistor count. He remains long, while flagging that value is starting to diversify beyond Nvidia into networking, power, and specialized silicon.
auth.lb:
Visser made Nvidia the face of the AI revolution early – the chip company whose stock surged as investors realized its hardware powers practically every AI data center. Through 2025 that bet paid off handsomely as the training boom drove Nvidia's market cap above $3 trillion, and it remains his most-covered name across more than a dozen reports.

But the view evolved rather than stood still. By late 2025 he argued the easy phase was ending: the constraint was moving from buying the most chips to moving data efficiently through power and memory. When Nvidia licensed Groq's inference IP and hired Google's original TPU architect, Visser read it as Nvidia admitting the next battle is edge inference – putting brains in phones, cars, and robots – which plays by different physics than the cloud.

Where it stands now: still structurally long, with Blackwell shipping in volume and Vera Rubin coming, but inside a broadening story. Visser increasingly spreads the opportunity to networking, memory, power semiconductors, and packaging. What would change the call is a genuine demand stall – but his recurring evidence (insatiable customer demand, sold-out supply chains) points the other way.
auth.st: Most-covered name; +73.7% since the March 2025 call (118 to 205). Blackwell ramping with over $1T in cumulative Blackwell/Vera Rubin orders through 2027; the variant is that the marginal constraint shifts from GPU supply to tokens-per-watt, memory bandwidth, and edge-inference economics.
auth.mt:
Nvidia underpins Visser's AI-compute book and screens as his highest-conviction, most-discussed long (+73.7% from the 24 March 2025 anchor). The bull specifics are concrete: Blackwell is ramping in volume with more than $1 trillion in cumulative Blackwell and Vera Rubin orders booked through 2027, and Nvidia's networking revenue grew 142% year over year in fiscal 2026 on system integration. The Rubin platform is engineered for agentic AI and large-scale MoE inference at up to 10x lower cost per token, with third-gen NVLink and inference context memory storage.

The nuance – and where conviction wobbled mid-2025 – is that Visser sees the value chain de-concentrating. The Groq licensing deal signals that the moat is migrating from CUDA lock-in and HBM supply control toward memory-compute co-design for edge inference. Vision-language-action models and embodied AI diffuse bottlenecks into optics, sensors, power, and batteries. Importantly, his thematic work is explicitly 'not a call against Nvidia' – Rubin validates the inference-efficiency thesis – but it argues for owning the broader enabling layer alongside it.
auth.lt:
Nvidia is the spine of Visser's AI-compute coverage – 26 mentions across 14 reports, tagged high-conviction long, up 73.7% from the 24 March 2025 call (118 to roughly 205). The arc runs from 'the compute emblem' through a 'tokens-per-watt' moat to the edge/Rubin transition, and the writing tracks that evolution faithfully rather than simply cheerleading.

The bull case is built on hard demand signals. Blackwell is ramping in volume against more than $1 trillion in cumulative Blackwell and Vera Rubin orders through 2027; networking revenue rose 142% year over year in fiscal 2026; and Rubin is purpose-built for agentic reasoning and massive MoE inference at up to 10x lower cost per token. Surrounding reports reinforce the scarcity: a NAND producer demanding three years of prepaid cash, CoWoS packaging booked out through 2026, and a Vera Rubin GPU needing over 20 terabytes of SSD per unit – one architecture potentially absorbing ~20% of global flash output.

The honest counter-current is concentration and a shifting constraint. Visser notes the market's mental model is anchored in the LLM paradigm where 'Nvidia, TSMC, and a handful of memory suppliers captured the majority of value because compute was the bottleneck' – and that VLAs and embodied AI 'shatter this concentration,' diffusing value into optics, power, memory controllers, and sensors. His mid-2025 wobble ('power is the new story') and the Groq pivot both say the same thing: the next dollar of value may not accrue to Nvidia alone. He is explicit it 'is not a call against Nvidia' – Nvidia's own roadmap validates the inference thesis.

Net read: the position has worked and the conviction is intact, but it is increasingly a 'Nvidia-plus' thesis – own the emblem, but spread into the broadening enabling layer of networking, power semis, and specialized inference silicon as the buildout moves from training to inference and from cloud to edge.

auth.sb: AMD is Visser's way to play two AI shifts at once: more distributed inference (where Lisa Su says AMD's GPUs shine) and the agentic era's revival of CPU demand. A supporting-cast name in his stack, not a marquee call.
auth.mb: AMD appears in Visser's notes as a beneficiary of where AI compute is heading rather than a standalone thesis. He cites CEO Lisa Su's point that reasoning models make inference more important and push toward distributed inferencing – “which plays to our strengths.” Separately, his agentic-AI work leans on Intel's CFO line that “the CPU has become cool again,” because orchestration around the model – scheduling, state, security, workflow – runs on CPUs, a tailwind AMD shares. He also tracks AMD gaining traction with its MI-series accelerators as a credible number-two to NVIDIA.
auth.lb:
AMD threads through Visser's AI-hardware mapping as a credible second source to NVIDIA and a play on how the workload mix is evolving. In his inference-inflection work, he quotes Lisa Su directly: reasoning models make inferencing more important, there is a shift toward more distributed inferencing, and that plays to AMD's strengths.

The agentic chapter widens the case. As AI moves from answering prompts to acting inside companies, the orchestration layer – scheduling, state management, file access, API coordination, security, monitoring – runs largely on CPUs. Visser uses Intel CFO David Zinsner's “the CPU has become cool again” to mark the turn, and AMD sits on both sides of that ledger with GPUs and CPUs.

On the accelerator side he flags AMD gaining traction with its MI series. The honest read is that AMD is a supporting name in his stack, not a high-conviction single-stock call – it benefits from the same demand wave that drives his bigger themes, with the competitive risk of running second to NVIDIA's entrenched ecosystem.
auth.st: Two-sided AI compute exposure: GPU upside from the shift to distributed inference (Lisa Su: “plays to our strengths”) plus CPU tailwind from agentic orchestration (Zinsner: “the CPU has become cool again”). MI-series gaining traction as the #2 accelerator; a thematic supporting name, not a marquee call.
auth.mt: Visser positions AMD across two structural shifts. First, the training→inference transition: he cites Lisa Su that reasoning models make inferencing more important and drive “more distributed inferencing,” which she frames as playing to AMD's strengths – and he notes the MI-series accelerators gaining traction as the credible alternative to NVIDIA. Second, the chatbot→agentic shift: the orchestration layer around the model (scheduling, state management, file access, API coordination, security, monitoring) runs mostly on CPUs, captured by Intel CFO Zinsner's “the CPU has become cool again” and his observation that the demand pickup began in H2 2025. AMD is dual-exposed to both legs via GPUs and CPUs. The realistic caveat: it is a thematic, supporting-cast name in the 100-stock stack rather than a deeply modeled high-conviction long, and it carries the structural risk of competing against NVIDIA's entrenched cloud-to-edge ecosystem.
auth.lt:
AMD sits in Visser's notes as a multi-vector beneficiary of the AI compute transition rather than a standalone, high-conviction call – it appears a handful of times, always thematically. The first vector is the inference inflection. In his “Inference Inflection” piece he quotes Lisa Su that with new reasoning models “inferencing is becoming more important” and there is “a shift toward more distributed inferencing, and I think that plays to our strengths” – positioning AMD where the workload growth is, not just in training.

The second vector is the agentic era's revival of the CPU. As AI moves from responding to prompts to acting inside companies, the orchestration around the model – scheduling, state management, file access, data prep, API coordination, security, monitoring, workflow logic – runs largely on CPUs. Visser anchors this with Intel CFO David Zinsner's “The CPU has become cool again” and the note that demand picked up in the second half of 2025. AMD's dual GPU-plus-CPU footprint means it participates in both the orchestration and the inference legs.

On the accelerator side, he flags AMD “gaining traction with its MI series accelerators,” treating it as the most credible challenger to NVIDIA in data-center GPUs as the buildout broadens beyond a single supplier.

The honest synthesis: AMD is a supporting name in the 100-stock thematic stack, riding the same secular demand that powers his bigger calls rather than carrying a differentiated single-stock thesis. The principal risk is competitive – NVIDIA's entrenched cloud-to-edge ecosystem and software moat – and AMD's outcome is largely a derivative of whether the broad inference-and-agentic buildout continues at the pace Visser expects.

pick.note: The 'factory that manufactures intelligence'; advanced-packaging scarcity.

auth.sb: The factory that manufactures intelligence – every AI chip flows through TSMC, and its CoWoS advanced packaging is the hard chokepoint, booked out through 2026. Long pick, up ~176%.
auth.mb: Visser treats TSMC as the irreplaceable toll booth of the AI buildout, but the real edge of his call is narrower: advanced packaging. CoWoS capacity is scaling from roughly 30,000 to 100,000–127,000 wafers per month by late 2026 and is fully booked through 2026 – which he reads as a "hard signal that demand remains structural rather than speculative." It is the enabling layer that validates demand again at each earnings print, a live catalyst rather than a distant secular story.
auth.lb:
TSMC enters Visser's coverage in April 2025 as the obvious foundry leader in the embodied-AI hardware buildout – the company every GPU and accelerator must pass through. Called near $155 on 3 April, it became one of his strongest performers.

Over the following year the thesis sharpens from "foundry leader" to a specific chokepoint argument. The binding constraint in AI isn't transistor density anymore – it's CoWoS advanced packaging, the step that stitches compute and high-bandwidth memory together. TSMC's published capacity ramp, booked solid through 2026, is the cleanest evidence in his whole framework that AI demand is structural.

What changes it: a break in the packaging chokepoint narrative – capacity catching up to demand, or a demand air-pocket in accelerators. For now Visser positions TSMC at the center of the "enabling layer" alongside Broadcom and Marvell, with its earnings dates flagged as catalysts that can "validate demand again."
auth.st: Long, high conviction. Anchored ~$155 (3 April 2025), current ~$428, return +176.1%. Theme: AI Compute & Semis. Edge of the thesis is CoWoS scarcity – capacity ramping 30k → 100k–127k wafers/month by late 2026, fully booked through 2026.
auth.mt: The mechanics: TSMC is positioned as the manufacturing-and-packaging choke point in the "heterogeneous AI factory," and Visser's specific, datable signal is the CoWoS ramp – ~30,000 wafers/month scaling to 100,000–127,000 by late 2026, fully booked through 2026. He uses booked capacity as a demand-confirmation mechanism: a sold-out forward book is harder to fake than sell-side optimism. In his March 2026 catalyst-chain framing, TSMC's 10 March print is one node in a live sequence (Broadcom/Marvell earnings → TSMC → Synopsys → Nvidia GTC) that can "validate demand again," making it actionable now rather than merely thematic. The honest caveat embedded in his own work: he stresses this is "not a call against Nvidia" – the packaging thesis is additive, and TSMC is exposed if the AI accelerator cycle's demand assumptions crack.
auth.lt:
TSMC appears in five reports from April 2025 to March 2026 as a consistent high-conviction long and one of Visser's best-performing picks (+176.1%). The through-line is that it is the indispensable manufacturer of AI silicon – "the factory that manufactures intelligence" – and that the scarce, value-capturing node has moved from raw fabrication to advanced packaging.

The April 2025 origin places TSMC among the picks-and-shovels of the embodied-AI buildout. By January 2026 the argument is concrete: CoWoS is named explicitly as "the chokepoint for AI chip production," with the capacity ramp and full 2026 booking cited as hard evidence of structural demand. By March 2026 TSMC is slotted into the heterogeneous-AI-factory thesis alongside custom-silicon names (Broadcom, Marvell) and EDA (Cadence, Synopsys) as the manufacturing/packaging leg of the enabling layer.

The risk Visser is most exposed to is the one he half-acknowledges: the whole edifice rests on AI accelerator demand staying structural. CoWoS being booked out is only bullish until it isn't – a demand air-pocket would hit the most cyclically-geared node hardest. He explicitly hedges that the thesis "is not a call against Nvidia," which signals the dependency: TSMC rides the same accelerator wave. Geopolitical/Taiwan concentration risk goes unmentioned in these passages, a notable omission.

Performance read: this is the cleanest win in the batch. The +176% return is grounded in a genuinely datable, repeatedly-confirmed catalyst (the packaging book) rather than pure narrative, which gives the call unusual durability among Visser's AI-regime expressions.

auth.sb: ASML is Visser's EUV-monopoly chokepoint long – the unbreakable bottleneck no advanced chip can be made without. The pick is up roughly 195% from the April 2025 call.
auth.mb: ASML is the high-conviction toll-booth on advanced fabrication: every leading-edge logic and memory node has to pass through its EUV lithography, making it a structural chokepoint rather than a cyclical tool stock. Visser flags it repeatedly as a key supply-chain bottleneck for advanced chips, with demand pulled forward as testing and tools surge alongside the AI buildout. Called long in April 2025 around $650, it has run to roughly $1,920 – a ~195% gain. The thesis is the monopoly, not the cycle.
auth.lb:
ASML's role in Visser's work is simple and durable: it makes the machines that make the most advanced chips, and nobody else does. As AI pulled the whole semiconductor supply chain forward, ASML's EUV lithography became the named bottleneck for advanced nodes – an unbreakable bottleneck and a toll-booth on every leading-edge fab.

The evolution is that the AI cycle keeps widening the set of customers needing the most advanced silicon. As inference demand scales 100–1000x and content per chip rises, demand for ASML's tools gets pulled forward, with the company flagged as a second-order beneficiary as testing and tools surge.

What changes it is the same thing that built it: monopoly economics are durable but lumpy. The pick, made in April 2025 near $650, has compounded to roughly $1,920 – about 195% – validating the chokepoint frame. The watch-item is order timing and any geopolitical limits on where the most advanced tools can ship.
auth.st: EUV monopoly = single-source chokepoint on all leading-edge logic and memory; demand pulled forward as inference scales 100–1000x. Pick called April 2025 ~$650 → ~$1,920, +195.4%.
auth.mt: Mechanistically, ASML is the only supplier of EUV lithography, so it functions as a single point of control over advanced-node throughput – Visser's 'unbreakable bottleneck.' The notes tie its demand directly to the inference curve (projected to scale 100–1000x, every chip generation delivering higher tokens-per-watt) and to the broader bottleneck stack: alongside DRAM, HDDs, transformers, turbines and cooling, ASML (EUV) is named as key for advanced chips. It is also classed as a second-order beneficiary that sees demand pulled forward as testing and tools surge. The performance is the variant-proof: from the April 2025 long at ~$650 the name is at ~$1,920, a +195.4% return – the chokepoint thesis paying out, not just describing.
auth.lt:
ASML is one of Visser's cleanest high-conviction longs, and the pick metadata confirms it: a 'long,' 'high' conviction, arc described as EUV monopoly – unbreakable bottleneck / toll-booth on advanced fab, called 3 April 2025 at ~$650 and now ~$1,920, a +195.4% return. It recurs as a 'chokepoint' name across the coverage.

The thesis is structural. ASML's EUV monopoly makes it the gate every advanced logic and leading-edge memory node must pass through. As the AI buildout pushes inference demand up 100–1000x and pulls the entire tool and test layer forward, ASML benefits without having to win a product race – there is no alternative supplier. Visser repeatedly slots it into the supply-chain-bottleneck discussion next to DRAM, power and cooling, with power availability now the top data-center siting constraint.

The honest risks: the stock has already tripled-plus off the call, so much of the chokepoint premium is priced; ASML's revenue is order-lumpy and capex-sensitive at the margin despite the monopoly; and the most advanced tools sit squarely in geopolitical crosshairs, where export restrictions can cap the addressable demand. The notes acknowledge widespread shortages and bottlenecks but do not stress-test ASML's downside.

Performance read: this is the standout winner in the batch on a tracked-return basis (+195.4%). The framing has been right and the price has confirmed it; from here the work shifts from thesis discovery to managing a name that is now richly valued on a moat the market fully sees.

pick.arc: HBM/DRAM oligopolist → 'memory shortage' winner → consensus after a 700% move
pick.note: His single best call. Long since spring 2025 on structural HBM/DRAM scarcity; rode it from ~$69 to four figures. By May 2026 he flags it's now consensus.

auth.sb: Micron is Visser's single best call – a long since spring 2025 on structural memory scarcity that ran roughly 14x. By May 2026 he flags the obvious risk: it's now consensus.
auth.mb: The thesis was simple and early: AI compute is starved for memory, and only three firms – Micron, SK Hynix, Samsung – make the high-bandwidth DRAM that feeds the beast. Visser called the structural HBM/DRAM shortage in April 2025 and rode Micron from the high-$60s into four figures as scarcity-driven hardware re-rated while abundance-era software fell. The catch by mid-2026 is that the parabolic chart everyone now points to as a tell of strength is exactly what makes the trade crowded. The mechanism still works; the surprise is gone.
auth.lb:
Micron makes memory – the chips that hold and move data right next to an AI processor. When Visser started writing about it in April 2025, the AI story was all about the GPUs doing the math. He saw the bottleneck one layer over: those chips are useless without enough fast memory to feed them, and high-bandwidth memory (HBM) is made by just three companies. That oligopoly plus exploding demand meant prices and profits could only go one way.

From there the trade compounded. As models grew, memory bandwidth became the binding limit, HBM swelled toward a fifth of the entire DRAM market by value, and the memory makers swung from losses to record profits. Micron went from the high-$60s to over $1,000 – a roughly 14x move that made it Visser's standout winner.

What changes the story now is its own success. By May 2026 Visser notes that Micron and the other scarcity winners have become the chart everyone cites – the thesis is no longer contrarian, it's the consensus. The structural shortage is real, but the easy money was made when nobody believed it.
auth.st: Three-supplier HBM/DRAM oligopoly (MU, SK Hynix, Samsung) levered to AI memory-bandwidth scarcity; called 3 April 2025 near $69, rode to ~$1,059 (+1,435%), flagged consensus by May 2026.
auth.mt: The April 2025 framing was mechanistic: HBM is stacked DRAM positioned next to the accelerator, delivering hundreds of GB/s per chip, and as model size grew, memory bandwidth – not raw FLOPs – became the critical limiter. With only SK Hynix, Samsung and Micron producing it, and HBM projected to exceed 20% of DRAM market value starting 2024, the pricing setup was an oligopoly meeting a demand shock. By September 2025 Visser was citing tight DRAM supply benefiting all three plus second-order names (Teradyne, ASML) as testing and tools demand pulled forward. The variant view to respect: by January 2026 he uses Micron's parabolic chart as the *bull* case against 'cheap' software – investors who 'infer value in what has fallen and excess in what has run' have it 'exactly backward.' The risk he himself names in May 2026 is that the consensus has caught up to the call.
auth.lt:
Micron is the cleanest expression of Visser's AI-hardware-scarcity playbook, and his richest, most decorated call. The origin (3 April 2025) was a hardware-frontier thesis: feeding AI compute requires advanced memory, HBM is the bottleneck, and only three firms make it. He named the oligopoly explicitly and tied it to a structural demand boom – HBM heading toward 20%+ of DRAM value, makers swinging from losses to record profit. The entry near $69 and the ~$1,059 print represent a ~1,435% return, the standout of the batch.

The thesis deepened rather than drifted. Through September 2025 he reinforced tight DRAM supply as a benefit to Micron and a ripple to testing/tools names. In October 2025 his NPU/edge work extended the memory-everywhere logic – intelligence becoming local infrastructure implies memory demand broadening beyond the data center. By January 2026 Micron had become his rhetorical anchor for the whole scarcity-over-abundance trade: the parabolic chart was proof the market rewards bottlenecks, not falling multiples.

The honest performance read: this was right, early, and big – his best work in the period. The honest risk read comes from Visser himself. By May 2026, in 'AI's Strait of Hormuz,' the frame inverts – the danger is no longer weak AI demand but demand so strong the physical supply chain (HBM included) can't keep pace, a narrow choke point that synchronized global scrambles can disrupt. And he flags the trade is now consensus after a ~700% (then larger) move. A crowded, parabolic memory name carries cyclical-peak risk: DRAM is historically a cyclical, and the very supply that justified the move eventually arrives.

Net: a marquee structural winner whose mechanism remains intact but whose asymmetry has narrowed sharply. The work to do now is distinguishing durable HBM scarcity from ordinary DRAM cyclicality – the place where a 14x winner becomes a round-trip if conviction outlasts the setup.

quotes.q: Investors look at the parabolic charts of scarcity-driven hardware winners like Micron and SanDisk and compare them to the abundance-era drawdowns of Salesforce, Adobe, and ServiceNow.
From that comparison, they infer value in what has fallen and excess in what has run. I think they have it exactly backward.
Tight DRAM supply benefits Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron, while bottlenecks ripple downstream.
SK Hynix and Samsung (in Asia) and Micron Technology (MU) (US) form an oligopoly in advanced memory.
As models grow in size and complexity, memory bandwidth has become a critical limiter – and HBM provides a solution with bandwidth on the order of hundreds of GB/s per chip.
For more than a year, I have felt comfortable staying with the AI capex trade and looking through the constant fears around AI demand and return on invested capital.

auth.sb: Broadcom is Visser's custom-silicon-plus-networking pick that more than doubled – the ASIC and optical enabler of the AI factory. By mid-2026 he's cooled as expectations got rich.
auth.mb: When AI clusters scale to thousands of chips talking at once, the network becomes the constraint, and Broadcom designs the custom ASICs and switching that handle the bandwidth. Visser flagged it in April 2025 as a core inference-scale-out enabler, supplying Google's TPU interconnect and AWS's custom silicon. The thesis matured into a concrete earnings story by March 2026 – Broadcom and Marvell had already 'moved the earnings math' toward visible AI revenue. But by June 2026 he marks the regime shift from recognition to execution: with everyone now seeing the same picture, surprise no longer pays.
auth.lb:
Broadcom does two things AI needs badly: it designs custom chips for the giant cloud companies, and it makes the networking gear that lets thousands of AI chips talk to each other at once. Visser put it on his hardware-frontier list in April 2025, noting it already supplies Google's TPU interconnect and Amazon's custom silicon – picks-and-shovels exposure to whoever wins.

Through early 2026 the call got more concrete. As agentic AI raised the bar on networking – copper wires and thin bandwidth couldn't keep up – Visser argued the enabling layer of the 'AI factory' was the place to own: Broadcom and Marvell for custom silicon, alongside the manufacturing and design-tool chokepoints. Crucially, he stressed this was 'actionable now,' because Broadcom had already moved real earnings, not just a five-year vision.

What changes it: the crowd caught up. By June 2026 Visser's 'fireworks' framing says the sky is fully lit – everyone sees agentic AI is real, so the burden of proof shifts from recognition to execution. Broadcom remains a large multi-quarter winner, but with the move from ~$165 to ~$383 already banked and expectations rich, the easy upside is behind it.
auth.st: Custom AI ASICs (Google TPU interconnect, AWS silicon) plus dominant networking/switching for inference scale-out; called 3 April 2025 near $165, ran to ~$383 (+132%). Earnings math already moved by early 2026; cooled as estimates richened.
auth.mt: The mechanism is the networking wall: training and inference across thousands of accelerators is bottlenecked by interconnect, and Broadcom designs the switch ASICs and PHYs that move massive bandwidth, plus bespoke silicon for hyperscalers. April 2025 framed it as a core enabler 'of inference scale-out' and explicitly the supplier behind Google's TPU and AWS custom chips. By March 2026 the thesis sharpened into a live catalyst chain – Broadcom and Marvell for custom silicon, TSMC for packaging, Cadence/Synopsys for the rising 'complexity tax' – with the key point that Broadcom and Marvell 'have already moved the earnings math,' making it actionable rather than thematic. The variant/cooling view is explicit in June 2026: at CES the market was 'still learning the language,' by Computex it 'already knew' it; once everyone sees the same picture, upside must come from execution, not recognition. That is the analytical case for a more neutral stance after a +132% run.
auth.lt:
Broadcom is Visser's high-conviction enabling-layer pick within AI semis – not the headline GPU, but the custom silicon and networking that make large clusters work. Origin: April 2025, where he grouped it with Marvell as designers of switch ASICs and network interface chips, noting it already supplied Google's TPU interconnect and AWS's custom needs. The entry near $165 against a current ~$383 is a +132% gain – a large multi-quarter winner, if not in Micron's league.

The thesis evolved from thematic to catalyst-driven. By March 2026's 'OpenClaw' piece, agentic AI was the demand accelerant raising the networking wall – he cited Meta's up-to-$6B Corning optical-fiber deal as evidence the bottleneck was real – and argued for owning the enabling layer: Broadcom and Marvell for custom silicon. The differentiator versus a pure secular story was that earnings had already moved, making it 'actionable now.'

The risk and performance read is candid. Through 2025 the stance was consistently bullish; by June 2026 it dropped to a mention, with the 'fireworks show is over' framing signaling a regime change. The risk is precisely embedded in the win: when agentic AI demand is universally assumed, a custom-silicon name priced for continued upside has to deliver execution, and any disappointment in hyperscaler ASIC ramps or networking share cuts both ways. There is also concentration risk – the custom-silicon thesis leans on a handful of hyperscaler customers whose internal-chip strategies can shift.

Net: a well-timed, well-reasoned winner that compounded as the AI-factory thesis went mainstream. Visser's own cooling is the most useful signal – not a sell, but an acknowledgment that the asymmetry has narrowed as recognition gave way to expectation.

auth.sb: Arista is Visser's go-to networking pick-and-shovel for the AI buildout – the high-speed switching that connects GPU clusters and, increasingly, the inference fabric. Bullish mention, no formal pick.
auth.mb: Arista shows up wherever Visser maps the AI data-center supply chain: it is the top networking name for connecting the thousands of chips that have to talk to each other at extreme speed inside training clusters and hyperscaler inference deployments. He pairs it with optics and custom silicon (Broadcom, Marvell, Ciena) as the connectivity layer enabling AI scale-out, and flags that Arista has been winning switching share from Cisco in exactly the AI-cluster niche that matters.
auth.lb:
Arista is a consistent bullish supporting name in Visser's AI-infrastructure maps, appearing three times but never elevated to a formal pick.

The role is clear and stable. When AI is deployed at scale, hundreds or thousands of chips must communicate at very high speed; that drives a wave of investment in high-performance networking, and Arista is named as the top player supplying switches to hyperscalers building AI clusters. In April 2025 he lists it as a pure-play way to own the cloud buildout; by May 2025 it sits in the networking/optics leg of his "inference inflection" map.

What would change the call is the same thing that drives it: the durability of hyperscaler AI capex and Arista's ability to keep taking switching share from Cisco as the network re-architects around AI pods. It is a connectivity toll on the buildout – bullish, but framed as a basket name rather than a high-conviction single-stock bet.
auth.st: No formal pick; bullish mention. Positioned as the leading high-speed Ethernet/switching supplier for hyperscaler AI clusters – the interconnect that lets thousands of GPUs train in parallel – and a share-gainer vs. Cisco in AI data-center switching.
auth.mt: The mechanism is interconnect bandwidth. Training large models runs hundreds-to-thousands of accelerators in parallel across servers, so cluster networking becomes a first-order bottleneck; Visser places Arista as the switching-gear leader connecting these AI pods, alongside InfiniBand (Nvidia/Mellanox) on the fabric side and Broadcom/Marvell on the switch-ASIC and interface-chip side. He explicitly notes the competitive vector: as the data center re-architects for AI, "Arista has been winning share" in the switching niche from Cisco. In the May 2025 inference map he groups it with Ciena, Marvell and Broadcom as the networking/optics layer enabling "ultra-low latency, high-bandwidth connectivity" for inference scale-out – the same physical-bandwidth constraint that later animates his optical-networking and Fujikura warnings.
auth.lt:
Arista appears in three reports (April–August 2025), uniformly bullish but never converted into a tracked pick – it is a basket/supply-chain name in Visser's framework rather than a conviction call. Its function is to occupy the networking node of the AI buildout: the gear that connects compute.

The thesis is mechanically sound and consistent across the reports. In the April 2025 hardware-frontier paper, Arista is the named switching supplier for hyperscaler AI clusters and an explicit share-taker from Cisco. The May 2025 "Inference Inflection" piece keeps it in the networking/optics group enabling real-time inference. The August 2025 earnings note doesn't single Arista out but sits it inside the broader "AI-driven infrastructure spending" surge that he argues is offsetting weakness elsewhere in the economy.

The risk profile is the standard pick-and-shovel set: Arista's fortunes are levered to hyperscaler capex continuing, and to it defending switching share against Cisco and merchant-silicon alternatives. Visser's later (2026) optical-bandwidth-wall and Fujikura-collapse warnings cut both ways here – they confirm networking/optics as a genuine chokepoint (bullish for connectivity demand) but also flag that the physical supply chain can disappoint violently when demand outruns it.

Honest read: there is no return to grade because Visser never formalized the pick. The takeaway is conviction-level – Arista is his preferred way to express the AI-networking leg, credible on share gains and structural demand, but he treats it as one name in a connectivity basket, not a standalone high-conviction bet.

auth.sb: Visser's highest-conviction AI-infrastructure long, and his cleanest call – the custom-silicon and optics name doubled in roughly eight weeks after he flagged it as a fresh pick.
auth.mb: Marvell sits at the choke point Visser cares most about: the interconnect and custom-silicon layer that the agentic-AI transition makes indispensable. He surfaced it first inside a May 2025 thematic inference basket, then promoted it to an explicit 'new pick' on 30 March 2026 near $155 – and it roughly doubled in eight weeks. The thesis is that as AI shifts from chatbots to agents, bandwidth, optics and custom silicon become the binding constraint, and Marvell owns that enabling layer rather than the crowded GPU trade.
auth.lb:
Marvell started life in Visser's notes as one item on a list. In May 2025 he was building an 'inference inflection' basket – the names that get real-time AI from trained models to deployed answers – and Marvell appeared as the custom-silicon-and-optical-interconnect entry alongside Broadcom, Arista and Ciena.

Over the next year that one-line mention hardened into a stand-alone conviction call. By March 2026 Visser argued the AI buildout had crossed a threshold: the move from chatbots to agents changes the economics of infrastructure, and the enabling layer – custom silicon, optics, the wiring between chips – becomes the trade rather than a footnote to Nvidia. On 30 March he named Marvell an explicit new pick near $155; it roughly doubled in about eight weeks.

What keeps the call alive is the demand picture: record revenue, management guiding for accelerating growth each quarter, and Nvidia's own roadmap pulling Marvell's components along with it. What would break it is the flip side of the same story – the AI supply chain is fragile, and Visser himself has warned that a single broken link can crack the whole bandwidth complex.
auth.st: Custom-silicon/optical-interconnect play on the agentic compute transition: Q4 FY26 revenue hit a record $2.219 billion, up 22% YoY, with management guiding FY27 growth above 30% (~$11bn) and FY28 near 40% (~$15bn) – compounding acceleration, not just growth.
auth.mt: The mechanism is the heterogeneous 'AI factory': as workloads move from training-heavy GPU clusters to agentic inference, the binding constraint shifts to moving data through memory, power and latency limits – the interconnect and custom-silicon layer Marvell supplies. Visser's specifics: Q4 FY26 revenue of $2.219bn (+22% YoY), FY27 guidance above 30% toward ~$11bn, FY28 near 40% toward ~$15bn, all above consensus. Nvidia is the tailwind, not the threat – Blackwell/Vera Rubin carry >$1tn cumulative orders through 2027, Nvidia networking revenue rose 142% YoY, and Rubin's NVLink and rack-scale architecture raise the value of best-in-class interconnect. The variant view he flags himself: the supply chain is the risk now, not demand – the Fujikura collapse was an early glimpse of how strong demand crashing into finite optical/HBM/power capacity can break a link and ripple.
auth.lt:
Marvell is the rare name where Visser made two distinct, dated, and trackable calls. The first was thematic: a May 2025 'inference inflection' basket where Marvell was the custom-silicon-and-optics entry, a position that compounded ~400% as the AI-networking trade ran. The second was a sharply timed stand-alone 'new pick' on 30 March 2026 near $155, which roughly doubled in eight weeks. Both worked, and the pick.ret of ~397% reflects the magnitude.

The analytical spine runs from his December 2025 Nvidia-Groq thesis: AI's next phase is decided not by who buys the most transistors but by who moves data through memory, power and latency most efficiently. The OpenClaw/agentic-software shift is the accelerant – when AI acts inside a company rather than answering a prompt, the enabling layer (custom silicon, optics, packaging) becomes investable now, not in five years. Marvell, with Broadcom, TSMC, Cadence and Synopsys, is the way to own that layer.

The fundamentals back the conviction: record $2.219bn quarter, accelerating guidance toward $11bn then $15bn, and Nvidia's Blackwell/Vera Rubin roadmap pulling Marvell's interconnect content along. Murphy's commentary turned visibly more assertive on bookings.

The honest risk is the one Visser himself raised weeks after the pick: the AI supply chain is fragile, demand may now be too strong for the physical world to keep pace, and Fujikura's post-earnings collapse showed how a single optical/HBM/power link can break and ripple. So far the call is a clear winner; the watch item is whether a supply-chain choke point turns the same demand surge into a downside catalyst.

auth.lb:

In that world, the chips that matter are NPUs, and Qualcomm's Snapdragon platform is already there. The mechanism: as AI workloads bifurcate into cloud training and edge inference, value shifts toward SRAM-centric, power-efficient architectures across the 1.2 billion smartphones, 80 million vehicles, and tens of billions of IoT devices shipped annually. Qualcomm's Snapdragon platform already embeds NPUs for on-device generative AI, and management has flagged edge inference growing rapidly in automotive and IoT.

Visser slots Qualcomm among the 'platform winners' that consolidate – firms controlling edge AI through silicon IP that gain pricing power and margin expansion in the inference era – alongside NVIDIA (cloud-to-edge), Apple (hardware ASPs), and Tesla (embodied AI). auth.lt:
Qualcomm is the embodied-AI/edge-inference expression in Visser's book – four reports, medium conviction, up 42.7% from the 15 May 2025 call (150 to ~214). The arc is simple and consistent: the edge-NPU leader across phones, auto, IoT, and eventually robots.

The mechanism rests on Visser's bifurcation thesis. The next chapter of AI unfolds at the edge, where NPUs turn every device category into an AI platform – a market spanning 1.2 billion smartphones, 80 million vehicles, and tens of billions of IoT devices annually that demands SRAM-centric, power-efficient silicon. Qualcomm's Snapdragon platform already embeds NPUs for on-device features, and its CEO has emphasized edge inference growing rapidly in automotive and IoT for a multi-billion-dollar market.

The bull specifics are structural rather than quarter-driven: Visser names Qualcomm among the platform winners (mobile/auto) that gain pricing power as edge AI consolidates, and his auto thesis – semiconductor content per vehicle climbing from hundreds to thousands of dollars, each car becoming a mobile inference hub – directly expands Qualcomm's addressable content. Humanoids and robotics are the long-tail optionality.

The honest read: the position has worked (+42.7%) and aligns cleanly with his highest-energy theme, but it is a medium-conviction, thesis-driven hold, not a flagged catalyst trade. The explicit risk is symmetric to the bull case – 'without NPUs scaling, AI remains cloud-constrained,' and the edge silicon buildout becomes a stranded asset.

Vertiv is Visser's clearest “plumber of the AI age” position – a medium-conviction long on the physical layer that converts capital and silicon into operating compute. The thesis sits inside his broader “physical world upgrade” argument: the AI buildout is constrained less by models than by power, electrical distribution and cooling, and the data center itself is being re-architected for AI. Because power has become, in his words, the number-one data-center siting constraint, the cooling-and-power vendors capture a structurally larger share of each new build than in prior cloud cycles.

The performance read is unambiguous: this has been one of the standout winners in the portfolio, up roughly 270% from the ~$85 call – a vindication of buying the infrastructure rather than chasing the chip leaders directly.

The risks are concentration and cyclicality. The position is a pure derivative of the AI data-center capex cycle; a genuine buildout slowdown, or an upstream “Flash wall” / supply bottleneck that stalls deployments, would remove the very demand the name monetizes. There is also limited stock-specific analysis in the notes – Vertiv is treated as a thematic vehicle for the power/thermal bottleneck more than a deeply modeled single name.

auth.sb: Visser's single highest-conviction macro call: own Bitcoin as the “purest AI trade” and a hedge on a Fed that can no longer afford to fight inflation. It is down hard in this window – he treats the drawdown as a chance to accumulate, not a reason to doubt.
auth.mb: Across 22 notes Visser keeps returning to one idea: a debt-soaked, financialized economy leaves the Fed trapped between defending jobs and crushing inflation, so every easing cycle comes sooner and every fight gets shorter – and that steadily erodes faith in fiat. Bitcoin is his answer, framed both as a monetary lifeboat and as the “purest AI trade,” a bottom-up rebellion against centralized capital that fits a world of autonomous AI agents needing native money. He never wavered through the slide, which has the position down about 21% from his call. He reframes the weakness as accumulation, not a broken thesis.
auth.lb:
Bitcoin is the thread that runs through Visser's entire body of work – the conviction he calls “the purest AI trade,” raised in roughly 22 of his notes and bullish in nearly all of them. The origin of the case is macro: a U.S. economy financialized to over 200% market-cap-to-GDP and carrying 122% debt-to-GDP simply cannot tolerate the kind of asset deflation a real inflation fight would require. So the Fed is structurally biased toward easing, and that bias slowly drains trust in government money.

The case then widens into AI. As autonomous agents begin to transact, Visser argues they need money that is native to machines – and Bitcoin sits at that intersection of monetary escape valve and digital-economy rail. He does not claim hyperinflation is coming. He only needs a world that increasingly expects every easing cycle to arrive sooner.

The honest read: in this window the trade has been a laggard, down about 21% from his roughly $82k anchor toward the 200-week moving average. Visser's response has been consistent – not to retreat, but to call the wash-out an accumulation zone. What would change the story is a credible return to fiscal discipline and a Fed free to stay restrictive; nothing in his notes suggests he expects that.
auth.st: Highest-conviction long: BTC as a fiat-debasement hedge on a Fed boxed in by 122% debt-to-GDP and >200% market-cap-to-GDP, plus optionality on machine-native money for the agentic economy. Currently a laggard – ~−21% from the ~$82k call, washed out near the 200-week MA, which he frames as accumulation.
auth.mt: The structural core of the thesis is the asymmetry of the Fed's reaction function. With debt-to-GDP at 122% and equity-market-cap-to-GDP above 200% (versus ~38–42% in the late 1970s), the central bank cannot defend price stability the way it once could without triggering intolerable asset deflation – so the market should price a lower easing threshold every cycle. Bitcoin monetizes that bias. Layered on top is the agentic-economy leg: as AI agents leave the chat box and begin executing financial transactions, Visser positions BTC within the “financial guardrails” of machine-native finance alongside stablecoins. The variant view is that he does not require hyperinflation for the trade to work – only an eroding belief in discretionary fiat management. The position is the portfolio's notable laggard in this window, down ~21% toward the 200-week MA, a drawdown he explicitly reframes as accumulation rather than thesis failure.
auth.lt:
Bitcoin is Visser's most-repeated and highest-conviction macro position – surfacing in roughly 22 notes, bullish in nearly all, and the one call he never softened even as it fell ~21% from his ~$82k anchor. The synthesis across reports rests on two pillars. The first is the “structural trap” he lays out in the D.O.G.E. 2.0 framework (Debt, Oil, Growth, Employment): a Fed facing supply-driven inflation inside a 122%-debt-to-GDP, >200%-market-cap-to-GDP economy cannot tighten the way it did in the 1970s without intolerable asset deflation. The conclusion is that the threshold for easing keeps falling – and a currency whose managers are biased toward debasement steadily loses credibility.

The second pillar is AI. Visser frames Bitcoin as the “purest AI trade” and a “bottom-up rebellion against centralized capital,” then extends it into the agentic economy: as agents leave the chat box to execute real financial work, they need money native to machines, and BTC sits in those “financial guardrails” alongside stablecoins (which he expects to explode post-GENIUS Act, with Ethereum as the settlement layer). This is what lets him treat a monetary hedge and a technology bet as the same position.

The disciplined caveat is his own: the thesis does not require hyperinflation. It only requires a world that increasingly believes every inflation fight will be shorter and every easing cycle sooner. That keeps the bar for being right low, but it also means the trade is a slow-burning regime bet, not a near-term catalyst play.

The honest performance read is that this has been a painful laggard in the window – washed out near the 200-week moving average while AI equities ran. Visser's response has been to reframe the drawdown as accumulation, consistent with his observation that good reward-to-risk setups are usually the ones investors dislike. The bear case is simply the mirror of his bull case: a genuine return of Fed independence and fiscal discipline would remove the debasement premium the whole thesis monetizes.

quotes.q: Bitcoin is the “purest AI trade” and a bottom-up rebellion against centralized capital
when combined with the rise of AI agents, Bitcoin is likely to become the defining story coming out of this new crisis
This four-part framework represents a structural trap for the Federal Reserve
if it must choose between defending employment and crushing supply-driven inflation inside a 122%-debt-to-GDP economy, markets should assume the threshold for easing is lower than in past cycles

Bitcoin does not need hyperinflation for this thesis to matter. It only needs a world in which the market increasingly believes that every inflation fight will be shorter, every easing cycle will come sooner

Second, more cautiously, as one of the handful of mega-caps (Nvidia, Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Amazon) where market gains have become dangerously concentrated – a "Blob" of names doing the bulk of the work while breadth narrows.
auth.lb:
Apple is a peripheral, no-pick name in Visser's notes – it appears four times, never as a thesis, always as supporting cast.

The constructive mention is structural: in his NPU framework (October 2025), Apple's Neural Engine is an example of the silicon that pushes AI inference onto the device, enabling offline transcription, summarization and assistant features. He lists Apple among the "platform winners" that consolidate through silicon IP and ecosystem control, monetizing via hardware ASPs as the edge-AI upgrade cycle unfolds.

The cautionary mention is macro: Apple is named as part of the over-concentrated mega-cap cohort driving narrow market gains, where the breadth that would mark a healthy bull is missing. Net, for Visser, Apple is a way to talk about edge inference and market concentration – not a stock he is recommending.
auth.st: No pick (mentions = 4). Role 1: on-device inference platform via the Neural Engine NPU – tagged a "platform winner" monetizing edge AI through hardware ASPs. Role 2: a constituent of the concentrated mega-cap "Blob" carrying index performance.
auth.mt: Mechanistically, Apple enters the thesis through the NPU layer: the Neural Engine in A-/M-series chips is cited (April 2025) as embedding on-device AI, part of a backdrop where ~30% of new smartphones were expected to ship with generative-AI capability and the device fleet (1.2bn+ phones/year) becomes the edge-inference substrate. In the October 2025 NPU paper Apple is grouped with NVIDIA, Microsoft, Qualcomm and Tesla as "platform winners" gaining pricing power through silicon IP and ecosystem lock-in, monetizing edge AI via hardware ASPs. The countervailing data point is the concentration risk: Apple sits inside Cembalest's "Blob" of mega-caps that, per Visser, account for "the bulk of equity market performance" – a feature he reads as fragility, not strength, and consistent with his standing view that the Mag7 will struggle and underperform as the rotation broadens.
auth.lt:
Apple is not a recommendation in any of its four appearances; understanding the call here means understanding why Visser leaves it on the sidelines. His central thesis is a regime shift from software/cloud AI to embodied/edge hardware AI, and within that, value migrates outward from the concentrated mega-cap winners toward sensors, packaging, power and physical-economy plays. Apple is structurally on the wrong side of that migration in his framing.

Where Apple does earn constructive billing is the edge-inference layer. The Neural Engine is his go-to example of NPUs putting "intelligence in billions of pockets," and he slots Apple among the platform winners that monetize on-device AI through hardware ASPs and ecosystem control. That is a real, mechanism-grounded positive – but it is generic to the device-upgrade thesis, not specific to Apple as a stock with a catalyst.

The risk frame is where Visser is most pointed. Apple is repeatedly cited as part of the over-concentrated mega-cap cohort – the "Blob" carrying the index – and he pairs this with an explicit view (August 2025) that the Mag7 "will have difficulties moving forward and will underperform" as breadth broadens into the S&P 493, small caps and industrials. That makes Apple, in his macro lens, more a source of concentration risk than an opportunity.

Honest read: there is no performance to grade because there is no pick. The signal investors should take is the absence itself – in a body of work brimming with high-conviction embodied-AI longs, Apple is conspicuously left as a mention, valued as a platform but not bought as a thesis.

auth.sb: Intel is a thematic mention, not a Visser pick – its relevance is the 'CPU has become cool again' angle: agentic AI needs orchestration, and orchestration runs on CPUs.
auth.mb: Visser never made Intel a conviction long; it appears as supporting evidence for two broader themes. First, the Nvidia–Intel partnership (September 2025) as proof the AI buildout is widening beyond GPUs. Second, and more pointed, Intel's CFO line that 'the CPU has become cool again' – because in the agentic phase, the orchestration layer (scheduling, state, APIs, security) runs on CPUs, not just the model. Intel is the messenger for a thesis, not the trade itself.
auth.lb:
Intel doesn't show up in Visser's notes as a stock he's banging the table on – there's no entry, no return, no conviction tag. It appears instead as a useful data point for bigger arguments.

The first is breadth: when Nvidia announced a partnership with Intel in September 2025, Visser read it as a sign the AI story was expanding from a one-or-two-winner GPU trade into 'compute and power expanding across many more players.' The second is sharper and more interesting. In March 2026 he quotes Intel's CFO saying 'the CPU has become cool again' – because as AI shifts from chatbots to agents that actually do things inside a company, you need orchestration: scheduling, file access, API coordination, security. Most of that runs on CPUs.

What would change it into an actual call is unanswered in these notes. For now Intel is the evidence, not the position – relevant because it signals where the agentic-AI bottleneck moves next, not because Visser is forecasting the stock.
auth.st: No pick – appears as thematic corroboration. Core mechanism cited: agentic orchestration (scheduling, state, API coordination, security) runs on CPUs, demand inflecting H2 2025 per Intel's CFO; plus the Sept 2025 Nvidia–Intel deal as breadth signal.
auth.mt: Intel is used to mark a workload shift, not to underwrite an equity thesis. The mechanistic claim (March 2026): in the chatbot era the model was the story; in the agentic era the system around the model – orchestration: scheduling, state management, file access, data prep, API coordination, security, monitoring, workflow – 'has to run on CPUs.' Intel's CFO David Zinsner is cited dating the demand pickup to the second half of 2025. Separately, the September 2025 Nvidia–Intel deal is framed as evidence that 2026 'won't just be about GPUs' but compute and power broadening across more players as the embodiment stage arrives. The April 2026 'Edge Awakens' note folds Intel into the power-semiconductor/edge convergence theme. Across all of these the stance is mention/bullish-theme, never a conviction long with an entry – the analyst is bullish the CPU-orchestration *idea* and agnostic on Intel the security.
auth.lt:
Intel carries no Visser pick – four mentions, all in service of themes rather than a position, and that distinction is the most important thing to convey. There is no entry price, no tracked return, no conviction rating.

The substantive idea Intel illustrates is the shift from chatbot to agentic AI and what it does to compute demand. In 'The Whole Rack' (March 2026), Visser uses Intel's CFO – 'The CPU has become cool again' – to argue that agentic systems need an orchestration layer (scheduling, state, APIs, security, monitoring) that runs on CPUs, with demand inflecting in H2 2025. This is a genuinely useful framing: it reopens a CPU role in AI that the GPU narrative had written off, and it is the strongest reason to look at Intel at all.

The second strand is breadth. The September 2025 Nvidia–Intel partnership is read as confirmation that the AI trade is broadening from a GPU-centric story toward 'compute and power expanding across many more players,' continuing into the embodiment stage. In April 2026's edge/power-semiconductor work, Intel sits alongside TI and Nvidia's 800V push as a signal that the next phase is about where intelligence happens and how it's powered.

The honest read: Visser likes the orchestration thesis but has not converted it into an Intel call – a meaningful absence given Intel's well-known execution and foundry risks, which the notes don't address. An investor should treat Intel here as a window into a workload trend (CPU-led orchestration, edge/power convergence) rather than a recommendation. The actionable takeaway is the theme – own the orchestration and power layers as agentic AI scales – with Intel as one possible, unendorsed, way to express it.

the orchestration away from just running the LLM has to run on CPUs.

The next phase of AI will be defined by where intelligence happens and how efficiently it is powered.

auth.sb: Visser's cleanest example that AI demand is real, not a bubble – Microsoft's rising capex, cloud revenue and order backlog feed each other in a self-reinforcing loop. The risk he flags is no longer too little demand, but too much.
auth.mb: Microsoft runs through Visser's notes less as a stock call than as the proof point for his “demand is real” argument. He calls it the cleanest example of a self-reinforcing spending loop: capex rises, supplier revenue rises today, the cost depreciates over years, order backlog (RPOs) signals future demand, and the race to secure frontier AI capacity accelerates. He also uses CEO Satya Nadella's framing – that AI agents are becoming “the new seats” and code a substitute for wages – to argue AI is a new form of liquidity that lowers the cost of work. In his edge-AI map, Microsoft is a platform winner monetizing software across the cloud-to-edge stack.
auth.lb:
Microsoft appears across Visser's work as evidence rather than as a single trade. In his “when real demand becomes the real risk” note, he holds Microsoft up as the cleanest example of why the AI-bubble argument may be missing the bigger danger: the market sees rising capex, rising cloud revenue, rising order backlog and a strategic race to catch frontier AI – all reinforcing each other.

He leans on Nadella's own language to make the macro point. When the CEO says coding is now a substitute for wages and “agents are the new seats,” Visser reads it as AI becoming a new form of liquidity – software replacing headcount the way cheap money once replaced effort, lowering the cost of work itself.

In the structural picture, Microsoft is one of the platform winners that consolidates power through software monetization and an integrated cloud-edge strategy as NPUs make AI profitable at the device level. Microsoft is also named among the handful of mega-caps driving the bulk of market gains – which is the bull case and, in his concentration warning, also the risk.
auth.st: Used as the reference case for genuine, self-reinforcing AI demand: capex → supplier revenue today → multi-year depreciation → rising RPOs → frontier-capacity race. Platform winner on software monetization across the cloud-to-edge NPU stack; flagged within the mega-cap concentration that carries the market.
auth.mt: Visser's Microsoft thread is mechanistic, not a target-price call. The “self-reinforcing loop” he describes: cash leaves today but the expense is spread over years, so accelerated tax deductions push capex forward; supplier revenue books immediately while the hyperscaler depreciates over time; remaining performance obligations (RPOs) make future demand visible; and the strategic race to secure frontier capacity accelerates the whole cycle. His conclusion inverts the bubble debate – with hyperscalers, enterprises, sovereigns and labs all treating AI capacity as strategically essential, the risk migrates from a demand shortfall to a demand surge. He reinforces the structural case with Nadella's “agents are the new seats” and “coding... a substitute for wages,” framing AI as balance-sheet liquidity that lowers the cost of work. In the edge-AI map, Microsoft is a platform winner via software monetization (Azure cloud-edge integration), and one of the concentrated mega-caps – NVIDIA, Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Amazon – accounting for the bulk of equity performance, a strength that doubles as concentration risk.
auth.lt:
Microsoft functions in Visser's notes as the load-bearing evidence for one of his central claims: that AI capex is backed by real, compounding demand rather than speculation. He calls it the cleanest example of a self-reinforcing loop in which rising capex, rising cloud revenue, rising remaining performance obligations and the race to catch frontier AI all feed one another. The accounting mechanics matter to him – cash leaves today but the expense depreciates over years, and favorable tax treatment only sharpens the incentive to pull spending forward – which is why he argues the demand-bubble case “may now be missing the bigger risk.” In his framing, the real exposure has flipped from too little demand to too much.

The second strand uses Microsoft's leadership to anchor a labor-displacement thesis. Drawing on Nadella's BG2 remarks that “coding is no longer a tool, it's more a substitute for wages” and “agents are the new seats,” Visser reframes AI as a new form of liquidity – software substituting for headcount, lowering the cost of work the way QE lowered the cost of money.

Structurally, Microsoft sits among his “platform winners” that consolidate pricing power through software monetization and integrated cloud-edge (Azure) strategies as NPUs make the GPU buildout profitable at the edge. He explicitly names it a beneficiary of the utilization flywheel that turns retraining demand into recurring returns.

The honest caveat is concentration. Visser repeatedly notes that a narrow set of mega-caps – NVIDIA, Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Amazon – accounts for the bulk of equity-market performance and corporate investment. That positioning is the bull case and, in his “concentration leaves most behind” warning, also the systemic risk: a market this dependent on a handful of names is fragile if the AI demand narrative ever cracks.

auth.mb: In Visser's notes Alphabet is a passenger on the AI-infrastructure trade rather than a standalone call. It is repeatedly grouped with NVIDIA, Microsoft, Apple and Amazon as the narrow set of firms accounting for the bulk of equity returns. The concrete pull is inference: Google Cloud inference workloads scaling rapidly on real-time generative demand, and Google LLM token usage cited up 50x year-over-year. auth.lb:
Alphabet enters Visser's work as part of a narrow group. It is named among the handful of mega-caps – NVIDIA, Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Amazon – that account for the bulk of equity market performance, which is both the strength and the vulnerability of its position.

The momentum is product and demand. Sundar Pichai is quoted on inference workloads scaling rapidly in Google Cloud on real-time generative demand; Veo 3 is flagged as AI crossing from chatbot to a system that begins to think, sense and reason; and Google LLM token usage is cited up 50x year-over-year – raw evidence of compute and power demand.

What changes the story is breadth. Visser's running concern is that the market's gains are concentrated in a narrow set of AI-tied mega-caps, Alphabet among them. As long as that concentration holds, Alphabet is carried; if the trade broadens or the bubble narrative bites, the same concentration that helped becomes the risk.
auth.st: Demand signal: Google LLM token usage cited up 50x year-over-year and Cloud inference workloads scaling rapidly – a token-volume tell, distinct from Alphabet's role as a concentrated index-carrying mega-cap.
auth.mt: The technical read on Alphabet is two-sided. On demand, the cited 50x year-over-year jump in Google LLM token usage and Pichai's commentary on inference workloads scaling rapidly mark it as an inference-monetization play, with Veo 3 (integrated audio, photorealistic video) extending that into multimodal sense-and-reason. The variant view embedded in the notes is concentration risk: Cembalest's 'Blob' report shows a handful of mega-caps – NVIDIA, Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Amazon – accounting for the bulk of market performance, so Alphabet's beta to the AI trade cuts both ways, and Visser frames the production function itself as having shifted to electrons-in, tokens-out.
auth.lt:
Across seven mentions Alphabet is consistently a supporting actor in Visser's AI thesis rather than a dedicated long; the only non-mention stance is a single 'bullish' inference piece, and there is no pick attached. The bull case is concrete: Pichai quoted on Cloud inference workloads scaling rapidly, Google LLM token usage cited up 50x year-over-year, and Veo 3 framed as AI moving from chatbot to a system that begins to think, sense and reason – evidence Alphabet captures both the demand and the product side of generative AI.

The second strand is positioning. Alphabet sits inside the narrow mega-cap cohort carrying the index, so it is simultaneously a buyer of NVIDIA-led compute and a seller of the intelligence that compute produces – attractive, but exposed to the trade it leads.

The honest risk is concentration. Visser's recurring frame – 'Bubbles Lift Everyone, Concentration Leaves Most Behind' – names Alphabet among the few firms carrying the market, quoting Mackay on men going mad in herds. That same narrowness is the vulnerability: a broadening or a deflation of the AI trade hits the concentrated leaders hardest.

Performance read: there is no tracked pick or return for Alphabet here. It is a high-quality way to own the AI demand curve in the notes, but Visser reserves his conviction calls (ASML, ENTG) for the picks-and-shovels layer, not the application mega-caps.

auth.sb: Amazon shows up in Visser's notes as evidence, not a pick – its leaked plan to replace 500,000 workers with robots is the headline proof that AI-driven labor displacement has moved from theory to execution.
auth.mb: Visser never made Amazon a call; he uses it as a recurring data point across three structural themes. The biggest is labor: Amazon's leaked internal plan to replace over 500,000 jobs with robots is his clearest signal that automation has moved from theory to execution and that corporate profits have decoupled from employment. Secondarily he cites Amazon as one of the handful of mega-caps driving concentrated market gains, and as a retailer with strong incentives to bypass card networks with its own stablecoin.
auth.lb:
Amazon appears in Visser's research as illustration rather than recommendation – there is no pick here, but the company anchors several of his macro arguments.

The most important is his Future-of-Labor thesis. He opens that report on Amazon's leaked plan to replace more than 500,000 jobs with robots, calling it the catalyst that moved the automation debate from theory to execution. It fits his broader observation that the historical link between S&P 500 earnings and payrolls broke down after ChatGPT launched, coinciding with the Mag7's hiring freeze since 2021 – earnings climb while job openings fall.

He also uses Amazon two other ways: as one of the narrow set of mega-caps (with Nvidia, Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet) accounting for the bulk of market gains in a concentration-not-bubble market, and as a giant retailer with billions in interchange fees and strong incentives to build its own stablecoin and bypass card networks entirely. Across all three, Amazon is a lens on Visser's themes – labor, concentration, and the machine economy – not a stock he is positioning around.
auth.st: Not a pick; used as the canonical evidence point for the post-ChatGPT decoupling of EPS from payrolls – the leaked plan to replace 500,000+ workers with robots is cited as the move from automation-as-theory to automation-as-execution.
auth.mt: Visser deploys Amazon as a structural data point across three mechanisms rather than as a positioned trade. (1) Labor decoupling: the leaked plan to replace 500,000+ jobs with robots anchors his argument that the EPS-to-payroll correlation broke after ChatGPT, with the Mag7 not hiring since 2021 – earnings rising as job openings and temp employment fall. (2) Market concentration: per Cembalest's 'Blob' work, Amazon is one of the few mega-caps (Nvidia, Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Amazon) carrying the bulk of equity performance and capex – his 'concentration, not bubble' frame. (3) Machine economy: as a retailer paying billions in interchange, Amazon has strong incentives to build its own stablecoin and bypass card networks, part of the agent-payments rails story. No arc, no return, no conviction tag – Amazon is the worked example in his macro slides.
auth.lt:
Amazon carries no Visser pick, and reading the passages straight, that is deliberate – it serves as evidence in his macro architecture rather than a name he sizes.

The load-bearing use is the Future-of-Labor report (October 2025), where Amazon's leaked plan to replace over 500,000 workers with robots is the literal catalyst – 'the debate over automation has moved from theory to execution.' He ties it to his recurring chart work: the S&P 500 EPS-versus-payroll correlation broke down after ChatGPT, the Mag7 has frozen hiring since 2021 (per Slok/Apollo), and earnings keep climbing as job openings and temp employment fall. Amazon is the most visible instance of a structural, not villainous, race to replace labor with digital workers under shareholder pressure.

Secondarily, in the concentration work (September 2025), Amazon is one of the narrow set of mega-caps accounting for the bulk of returns – Visser's argument that today's market is concentration, not a broad 1841-style mania. And in the machine-economy/stablecoin note (January 2026), Walmart and Amazon are flagged as retailers with the interchange-fee incentive to bypass card networks with their own tokens.

The honest read: there is nothing to grade here as a position. The value is interpretive – Amazon is Visser's repeatable shorthand for three of his biggest themes (labor displacement, market concentration, payment-rail disruption). An investor should treat these as context for the names he does pick, not as an Amazon thesis.
