auth.sb: Visser's macro long on Brazil – his way to play weak-dollar plus the rare-earth/clean-energy needs of embodied AI. New pick June 2025 near $28, up ~22%.
auth.mb: Brazil is Visser's regime-shift trade rolled into one ETF: three secular tailwinds converging – a structurally weaker dollar, the reordering of global trade and capital flows away from the US, and AI's pivot into a hard-asset supercycle that needs Brazil's minerals and clean power. He cites the historical leverage (a 1% DXY drop has driven MSCI Brazil up over 4%) and a constructive technical setup, while honestly flagging the fiscal overhang of public debt near 92% of GDP.
auth.lb:
Brazil is a personal, top-down conviction trade for Visser – he lived there and is "always looking for top-down reasons to get long it when it is off investor's radar." He made it a new pick on 3 June 2025 near $28.

The thesis braids three threads. The macro: the dollar's structural weakening and an Asia-repatriation dynamic that historically lifts high-yield EM currencies like the real – a 1% DXY drop has translated to ~2.5–3% BRL appreciation and 4%+ in MSCI Brazil. The AI angle: as the AI story leaves software for a capital- and energy-intensive hardware phase, Brazil's rare earths, lithium and clean power become strategic location advantages for compute. And the geopolitical: Brazil's balanced China/US trade position makes it a beneficiary of trade reordering.

What changes it is the dollar and the fiscal story. He concedes gross public debt near 92% of GDP is a real concern, but argues dollar weakness historically aids fiscal repair via inflows and lower imported inflation. Confidence comes on the technical breakouts he flags (USDBRL targeting 4.60, IBOV cup-and-handle). Up ~22% since the call – a medium-conviction macro expression, not an AI single-stock.
auth.st: Long, medium conviction. Anchored ~$28 (3 June 2025), current ~$34.19, return +22.1%. Theme: Macro & Fed. Drivers: weak DXY (1% DXY drop → ~4% MSCI Brazil historically), AI hard-asset/rare-earth supercycle, trade-flow reordering. Risk: gross public debt ~92% of GDP.
auth.mt: The quantified core is the dollar beta: Visser cites that over 22 years a 1% DXY decline has lifted MSCI Brazil over 4%, and 2.5–3.0% in the BRL – so EWZ is a high-convexity expression of his weak-dollar regime view. Layered on is the "hard-asset supercycle" mechanism: embodied AI's shift to a capital/energy-intensive phase (humanoids, FSD fleets, data centers "consuming more electricity than EVs") drives demand for the minerals and clean power Brazil supplies, with China already investing in its lithium/EV base and the US backing rare-earth supply via Ex-Im. The technical overlay (John Roque: USDBRL breaking its 40-week average toward 4.60, EWZ reversing a false breakdown into breakout territory; Visser's own Elliott Wave read of a completed 13-year BRL bear cycle) provides timing confirmation. The honest variant/risk he names himself: fiscal sustainability with public debt heading to 92% of GDP – a concern he argues is "not new" and historically eased by dollar weakness, but unresolved.
auth.lt:
EWZ is Visser's macro-regime trade and one of only two formal picks in this batch outside the AI semis/embodied-AI core. It appears in two June 2025 reports, with the new pick formalized 3 June near $28; it is up ~22.1%. He is explicit that it is a medium-conviction, personally-favored top-down idea rather than a high-conviction structural call.

The synthesis is unusually clean for a macro trade because three independent tailwinds point the same way. Currency: the dollar's structural weakening, amplified by the documented MSCI-Brazil-to-DXY beta. AI/commodities: Brazil as a mineral and clean-energy beneficiary of the embodied-AI hard-asset supercycle – his bridge from the AI thesis into EM. Geopolitics: trade and capital-flow reordering, with Brazil's balanced China-surplus/US-deficit position and inbound Chinese lithium/EV and US rare-earth investment making it a structural winner of a multipolar trade map.

The risk he is most candid about is fiscal: gross public debt near 92% of GDP. His rebuttal – that dollar weakness historically supports fiscal repair through inflows, lower imported inflation and better debt affordability – is plausible but leaves the trade hostage to the DXY thesis. If the dollar firms, the same convexity that drives the upside works in reverse, and the fiscal overhang re-asserts. The call also leans heavily on technicals (Roque's levels, Visser's Elliott Wave "13-year bear cycle complete" read) for timing, which is lower-confidence than the structural argument.

Performance read: the trade has worked, +22.1%, consistent with a weakening-dollar environment doing the heavy lifting. But it is a leveraged bet on a single macro variable wrapped in a commodity/AI narrative – the return validates the regime call so far, not the durability of Brazil's fiscal position. Medium conviction, correctly labeled.

over the last 22 years it has also led to MSCi Brazil rising over 4% for each 1% drop in the DXY.

I see Brazil as an example of a potential beneficiary, thanks to its critical rare-earth minerals and potential tailwinds from a weaker dollar.

auth.sb: Ethereum is a thematic bull, not a tracked pick – Visser's frame is that ETH becomes the settlement layer for stablecoins and tokenized real-world assets.
auth.mb: Visser is constructive on Ethereum as infrastructure rather than as a trade with an entry. His recurring thesis across mid-2025: stablecoin usage is set to explode post-legislation (Genius Act), and Ethereum is where that settles – the rails for tokenization and 'machine-native finance.' He pairs it with Bitcoin as 'economic lifeboats' against dollar pressure and centralized capital, and ties ETH to the rise of crypto-native startups that may never IPO. It's a structural-adoption call, not a price target.
auth.lb:
Ethereum shows up in Visser's videos as a structural idea, not a position he's sized. There's no pick, no entry, no tracked return – he's bullish the role it plays, not calling a level.

That role is settlement. Across June and July 2025 he argues stablecoins are about to go mainstream once legislation (the Genius Act) clears, and Ethereum is the layer they settle on. Tie in real-world-asset tokenization – stocks, bonds, funds moving onto crypto rails – and ETH becomes the plumbing for a machine-native financial system. He frames July legislation as a possible inflection point for adoption.

The bigger backdrop is his macro view: a weakening dollar, falling labor share, and capital concentrating in AI – conditions where Bitcoin and Ethereum act as 'economic lifeboats.' He also flags that future leaders may raise crypto-native capital and never go public, which would let value exit public markets. None of this is a trade recommendation; it's a thesis about where the rails are heading, with ETH positioned as the winner of the settlement layer.
auth.st: No tracked pick – structural bull. Mechanism: ETH as settlement layer for stablecoins (post-Genius Act) and RWA tokenization; July 2025 legislation flagged as an adoption inflection; paired with BTC as macro hedge against dollar debasement.
auth.mt: Ethereum is a thematic, mention-stance position with no entry or return attached. The mechanistic claim is consistent: stablecoin usage 'set to explode post-Genius Act' with 'Ethereum the settlement layer,' and ETH 'leads RWA tokenization and stablecoin infrastructure.' Visser nests this inside a macro regime view – a PBOC-flagged 'end of dollar dominance,' falling labor share of income since the 1980s now accelerated by AI, and capital winning over labor – making Bitcoin and Ethereum 'economic lifeboats' and 'machine-native finance' the new rails. A distinctive second-order point: he argues tokenized, crypto-native startups 'may never IPO,' so 'public markets could underperform long term as capital exits' – an implicit relative-value case for owning the settlement layer over the public-equity index. The honest gap: there is no figure, valuation, or catalyst-dated price view for ETH itself – conviction is on adoption, not on the token's near-term return.
auth.lt:
Ethereum carries no Visser pick – three mentions, all within weekly-video macro pieces, all bullish on the theme but none establishing a tracked position. That framing matters: there is no entry, no return, no price view, only a thesis about ETH's structural role.

That role is the settlement layer of tokenized finance. From June through July 2025 Visser repeatedly argues stablecoin usage will explode after the Genius Act and that Ethereum is where settlement happens, with ETH 'leading RWA tokenization and stablecoin infrastructure.' He flags July legislation as a potential inflection for crypto adoption. The mechanism is plausible and specific to ETH: if real-world assets and stablecoins move onto programmable rails, the chain that hosts that activity captures the value.

The macro scaffolding is where his conviction actually sits. He sees a falling labor share accelerated by AI, dollar pressure (citing a PBOC speech on the end of dollar dominance), and capital concentrating in AI-native firms – an environment where Bitcoin (his 'purest AI trade') and Ethereum function as economic lifeboats. He extends this with a structural warning for equity investors: crypto-native startups may never IPO, letting capital exit public markets, which would make owning the rails relatively more attractive than owning the index.

The honest read and risks: this is a directional, adoption-driven thesis with no price discipline, no timing on ETH specifically, and heavy dependence on legislation and tokenization actually scaling – none of which the notes quantify. ETH's competitive position (rival L1s, L2 fee compression, the question of whether settlement value accrues to the base token) is not addressed. For an investor, the actionable content is the theme – stablecoins and RWA settlement as a multi-year adoption wave with ETH as the leading candidate – rather than a sized recommendation. Treat it as a high-level structural bull case to underwrite independently, not a tracked Visser trade.

auth.sb: Circle is Visser's pure-play bet that stablecoins become the settlement rails for the AI-agent economy – a story he argues is not yet priced in. It's a thesis call on infrastructure, not crypto speculation.
auth.mb: Circle is framed as an infrastructure name, not a crypto trade: the convergence of autonomous AI agents and programmable digital dollars is, in Visser's words, about which financial rails carry the next decade of commerce. He explicitly argues stablecoin growth is not yet priced into the equity. The macro pull is large – industry estimates of trillions in deposits potentially migrating to stablecoin ecosystems, with Mastercard, J.P. Morgan, PayPal and even Walmart and Amazon building into the space. There is no formal pick attached, but the directional conviction is clear.
auth.lb:
Circle shows up as the equity expression of a bigger idea: AI agents need money that legacy bank rails cannot provide, and stablecoins are that money. Visser is blunt that filing this under crypto speculation is a category error – the real story is which rails carry the next decade of commerce.

The momentum is in the participants. Mastercard launched its Agent Pay protocol with Citi and US Bank piloting; J.P. Morgan advances Kinexys; PayPal's stablecoin grew from $1.2 billion to $3.8 billion in late 2025; and Walmart and Amazon, paying billions in interchange, have strong incentives to bypass card networks. Regulation is moving from 'whether' to 'how' with the CLARITY and GENIUS Acts.

What changes the story is pricing and policy. Visser's standing view is that stablecoin growth is not yet priced in – the upside case. The offsets: PayPal-style yield arrangements drawing regulatory scrutiny for contradicting stablecoin legislation, and the open question of whether retailers and banks crowd issuers like Circle rather than route through them.
auth.st: Pure-play on stablecoin settlement displacing card/deposit rails: banking-industry estimates of trillions in deposits migrating to stablecoin ecosystems; PayPal's coin scaled $1.2B → $3.8B late 2025. Visser's tell: growth 'not yet priced in.'
auth.mt: The technical case rests on an architectural incompatibility Visser argues no bank modernization can fix: AI agents transacting need programmable, instantly settling digital dollars, which legacy rails break on. The addressable shift is framed as trillions in traditional deposits potentially migrating into stablecoin-based ecosystems. Adoption data points are specific – PayPal's stablecoin growing from $1.2 billion to $3.8 billion in late 2025 (largely via DeFi integrations near 3.7% yields), Mastercard's Agent Pay with Citi/US Bank pilots, J.P. Morgan's Kinexys. The variant risk is also technical: that same ~3.7% yield drew regulatory scrutiny for contradicting the spirit of recently enacted stablecoin legislation, and retailer-issued coins (Walmart, Amazon) could route volume around an issuer like Circle. Visser's explicit edge claim is that stablecoin growth is not yet priced in.
auth.lt:
Circle appears across four notes, mostly 'mention' with one 'bullish' machine-economy piece, and no formal pick is attached – so the conviction is thematic rather than a tracked return. The core argument is that the convergence of autonomous AI agents and programmable digital dollars is an infrastructure story about financial rails, and that treating it as crypto speculation is a category error.

The demand thesis is concrete: legacy rails break down for agent-to-agent commerce, and banking-industry estimates suggest trillions of dollars in deposits could migrate into stablecoin ecosystems. Visser supports it with the roster of participants – Mastercard Agent Pay (Citi, US Bank pilots), J.P. Morgan Kinexys, PayPal's coin scaling $1.2B to $3.8B – and the regulatory shift via the CLARITY and GENIUS Acts from 'whether' to 'how.'

The risks are unusually well-flagged for a bull case. The ~3.7% DeFi yields driving PayPal's growth drew regulatory scrutiny for contradicting the spirit of recently enacted stablecoin legislation – a direct threat to the yield-led adoption flywheel. And the strongest incentive holders, Walmart and Amazon, are reportedly building their own stablecoin ecosystems to bypass card networks, which could mean they bypass third-party issuers too.

Performance read: no return is tracked. The standout claim is Visser's repeated assertion that stablecoin growth is not yet priced in, positioning Circle as an under-discounted infrastructure bet whose payoff depends on regulation landing favorably and on issuers capturing, rather than being disintermediated by, the volume.

Banking industry estimates suggest trillions of dollars in traditional deposits could migrate into stablecoin based ecosystems as this infrastructure matures.
PayPal’s stablecoin grew from $1.2 billion to $3.8 billion in late 2025, growth fueled largely by DeFi integrations offering yields around 3.7%.

auth.mb: Oracle's blowout backlog was the centerpiece of Visser's 'AI demand outstrips supply' thesis. auth.lb:
When Oracle reported a $317B quarter-over-quarter jump in AI orders in September 2025 – pushing backlog toward the mid-$400Bs, later framed toward $500B – Visser treated it as a watershed. Here was a real company committing real billions, proof that AI demand was massive and that the binding constraint was inference capacity, not training.

He leaned in hard, repeatedly. The striking thing to him wasn't just the number but the disbelief: even with 'insanely large evidence,' skepticism reigned, investors fixating on RPO accounting, margins, and the debt Oracle might take on rather than the signal. He argued the order surge made AI a macro force – lifting power, grid, industrials, and commodities as a tide lifts all boats.

Where it stands: a timing miss. The high-conviction call landed right after a sharp spike near $328, and the stock has fallen back (down roughly 9%). The thesis about demand may prove correct, but the entry point and the subsequent AI-bubble fears that dragged the whole software group down hurt the trade.

Oracle anchored Visser's supply-constrained-AI framework. The data point: a $317B increase in RPO orders quarter over quarter (up 359% YoY), pushing backlog toward $500B – which he sized at roughly 81% of US quarterly GDP and as estimated power needs equal to a large slice of US electricity demand. His two takeaways were that AI demand is massive and under-supplied, and that inference, not training, is the driver, with Ellison now negotiating at the CEO-and-head-of-state level.

The variant view he flagged repeatedly was the persistence of disbelief – investors discounting the print on RPO mechanics, margins, and financing risk rather than the demand signal. The honest read is a timing miss: the loud, high-conviction reiteration came mid-September 2025 right after a ~36% spike near $328, and the name is down about 9.3% since as AI-bubble fears re-rated the broader software complex lower into October.
auth.lt:
Oracle is the empirical keystone of Visser's 2025 'AI demand is outstripping supply' thesis – seven reports, high-conviction long, but a candid timing miss (down ~9.3% from the call). The arc: 'astronomical' AI demand, the $455B-class RPO inference shock, then backlog rationing.

The substance is the order book. Oracle reported a $317B QoQ surge in RPO (up 359% YoY), pushing backlog toward $500B – which Visser contextualized as roughly 81% of US quarterly GDP. For him the message was twofold and macro: AI demand is massive and capacity-short, and inference is the driver, not training. He explicitly told readers to 'forget what you think about Larry Ellison' and the financing questions; the signal was that businesses are already committing billions, elevating AI from a tech story to a driver of power, grid, and industrial build-outs (his PMI-rises-and-lifts-all-boats logic).

The recurring meta-observation is disbelief: even against overwhelming evidence, skepticism reigned, and the Oracle conversation 'died much faster' than the DeepSeek panic. Visser argued that denial wouldn't last, but warned that by the time recognition arrives the opportunity may be priced in – ironically prescient about his own entry.

The honest performance read: the thesis on demand looks directionally sound, but the trade was poorly timed. The loudest reiteration landed right after a ~36% spike (~$328), and Oracle's decline began in October alongside peak AI-bubble fears that dragged the whole software sector lower. Visser's own later framing – that names 'more closely tied to the AI buildout than to the disrupted software trade' could mark a tradable low – is effectively the rehabilitation case for the position.

auth.sb: Visser reframes Chevron from an oil giant into an 'electron giant' – a supplier of dispatchable gas-fired power for AI data centers. It's the long-energy leg of his long-energy/short-software pair, up about 26%.
auth.mb: Chevron is Visser's gas-to-power play on AI's power constraint. His pitch: Chevron and Exxon aren't oil giants anymore, they're electron giants for the AI age – uniquely able to supply the around-the-clock, dispatchable power that AI campuses need on a timeline nuclear can't match. The moat is vertical integration: gas reserves, pipelines, and balance sheets that can bundle fuel and generation into 20-year contracts. It is the long side of his long-energy / short-software pairing, and it's up roughly 26% since the June 2025 call.
auth.lb:
Visser's energy thesis flips the script on the supermajors. As AI compute decentralizes into warehouses, hospitals, vehicles, and factories, he argues it creates a vast, 24/7 demand for local, dispatchable power that can't tolerate latency or downtime – and natural gas is the only ready, scalable, geographically flexible answer while nuclear stays beyond 2030.

That makes Chevron and Exxon, with unmatched gas reserves, pipeline infrastructure, and modular gas-to-power deployments, the 'electron giants' of the AI era. The deeper moat, he stresses, is vertical integration – 'molecules to megawatts' as complete solutions, with the scale to allocate 400-500 MMcf/d to a single campus and balance sheets to underwrite 20-year contracts.

The positioning is a pair: long energy, short software. Visser repeatedly cites Chevron-versus-Salesforce as the trade turning in energy's favor – energy demand from AI surging while software margins face disruption. Up about 26% since June 2025, it's a medium-conviction structural bet; the arbitrage is the gap between cyclical-commodity pricing today and strategic-infrastructure-provider pricing tomorrow.
auth.st: Gas-to-power AI infrastructure play; +25.9% since June 2025. The long leg of a long-energy/short-software pair. Edge for vertical integrators able to allocate 400-500 MMcf/d to a single campus and underwrite 20-year fuel-plus-generation contracts; 10-20 GW of new AI capacity implies $70-100B of spend.
auth.mt:
Chevron is the long-energy leg of Visser's long-energy / short-software pair (medium conviction, +25.9% from the June 2025 call). The mechanism: embodied/edge AI generates a decentralized, 24/7, latency-intolerant compute load that needs local dispatchable power, and natural gas is the only scalable, geographically flexible solution before advanced nuclear arrives post-2030. The deeper moat is vertical integration – 'molecules to megawatts' delivered as complete systems, since orchestrating fuel logistics, generation, electrical, thermal, and controls from multiple vendors is operationally prohibitive at gigawatt scale.

The variant view is a repricing arbitrage: the gap between current cyclical-commodity multiples and the emerging reality of strategic infrastructure providers locked into multi-decade contracts. Visser sizes the prize at 10-20 GW of new AI capacity over five years implying $70-100B of infrastructure spend, with the scale to allocate 400-500 MMcf/d of gas to a single campus and balance sheets to structure 20-year bundled fuel-and-generation contracts. The recurring expression is relative: Chevron vs. Salesforce as the cleanest read on energy outperforming software.
auth.lt:
Chevron is the supermajor expression of Visser's power thesis – four reports, medium conviction, up 25.9% from the 17 June 2025 call (143 to ~180). The arc: gas-to-power for AI campuses, oil/gas reframed as an 'AI trade,' the long leg of a long-energy / short-software pair.

The mechanism turns on AI's power constraint. As compute decentralizes into a mosaic of new demand zones – warehouses, hospitals, homes, vehicles, factories – it creates 24/7, dispatchable-power demand that can't tolerate latency or downtime, often in places where solar and nuclear aren't feasible at scale and grid interconnection lags. Natural gas is the ready, scalable, flexible answer while advanced nuclear stays beyond 2030. Hence the reframe: Chevron and Exxon are 'electron giants' with unmatched gas reserves, pipelines, and modular gas-to-power deployments.

The bull specifics center on vertical integration as the real moat: 'molecules to megawatts' as complete solutions rather than discrete components, because orchestrating fuel logistics, turbines, switchgear, thermal, and controls from many vendors is operationally prohibitive at gigawatt scale. Visser quantifies the opportunity – 400-500 MMcf/d allocable to a single campus, 20-year bundled contracts, and 10-20 GW of new AI capacity implying $70-100B of spend – and frames the trade as an arbitrage between today's cyclical-commodity pricing and tomorrow's strategic-infrastructure pricing.

The positioning honesty: this is half of a pair, and Visser's clearest evidence is relative – 'Chevron vs. Salesforce performance turning in energy's favor,' driven by AI energy demand surging while software margins face disruption. Up ~26%, the long leg has worked; the bear risk is implicit (faster nuclear/grid build, or a software re-rate that breaks the pair), but the through-line across reports is a consistent, structurally argued long.

pick.arc: 'Molecules-to-megawatts' — gas + LNG powering data centers
pick.note: Reframed Big Oil as 'electron giants' / utility-like AI cash flows.

auth.sb: Visser's contrarian reframe: ExxonMobil isn't an oil giant anymore, it's an 'electron giant' selling dispatchable gas power to AI data centers – a utility-like cash-flow story the market still prices as a cyclical commodity.
auth.mb: Visser flagged ExxonMobil as a 'new pick' in June 2025 on a simple reframe – Big Oil is becoming Big Power. As AI's binding constraint shifts from chips to electricity, Exxon's natural-gas reserves, pipelines and modular gas-to-power deployments let it deliver around-the-clock, off-grid power to data centers on a timetable nuclear can't match. The 'molecules-to-megawatts' vertical integration – production, midstream and balance sheet to sign 20-year contracts – is the moat. The arbitrage is the gap between cyclical-commodity pricing today and strategic-infrastructure-provider reality emerging.
auth.lb:
In June 2025 Visser made the contrarian case in the title itself: Chevron and Exxon aren't oil giants anymore, they're electron giants for the AI age. The argument starts from where AI is going – not just centralized training clusters, but a decentralized, 24/7 layer of compute embedded in warehouses, hospitals, homes and vehicles that won't tolerate latency or downtime, and so needs local, dispatchable power.

Natural gas, in his telling, is the only ready, scalable, geographically flexible answer before advanced nuclear arrives post-2030. Exxon has the gas reserves, the pipeline infrastructure, and modular gas-to-power plants that can run independently of the utility grid for faster deployment – plus an LNG portfolio targeting 40 mtpa by 2030 to serve Europe and Asia. Management itself frames the company around 'escalating requirements for computing power in artificial intelligence.'

By January 2026 the thesis matured into a structural moat argument: vertical integration delivering 'molecules to megawatts' as complete solutions, with Exxon able to allocate 400-500 MMcf/d to a single campus and structure 20-year bundled contracts. Conservative math – 10-20 GW of AI capacity over five years – implies $70-100bn of spend. What changes the call is the same dependency: it's a medium-conviction reframe, and the moat holds only as long as gas stays the bridge fuel before nuclear and grid alternatives scale.
auth.st: Reframe from cyclical E&P to utility-like AI-power infrastructure: vertical integration ('molecules to megawatts') lets Exxon allocate 400-500 MMcf/d of gas to a single campus and structure 20-year bundled fuel-plus-generation contracts; 10-20 GW of AI capacity over five years implies $70-100bn of spend.
auth.mt: The mechanism is supplier value capture through vertical integration, not commodity beta. Gigawatt-scale AI power requires simultaneous orchestration of fuel logistics, generation equipment, electrical infrastructure, thermal systems and controls – 'plugging pieces together' from multiple vendors is operationally prohibitive, so the integrated supplier captures disproportionate value. Exxon's edge: production scale to allocate 400-500 MMcf/d to one campus, midstream to build dedicated pipelines, and a balance sheet to underwrite 20-year contracts bundling fuel and generation under unified accountability. Gas turbines are the most binding input – 100+ week lead times, oligopoly structure. The demand driver Visser emphasizes is embodied/edge AI: decentralized, latency-intolerant, 24/7 compute that needs local dispatchable power, with off-grid gas plants deploying faster than utility interconnection or post-2030 nuclear. The variant kicker is LNG – a 40 mtpa target by 2030 extends the same power-demand thesis to Europe and Asia. The arbitrage: cyclical-commodity pricing today versus strategic-infrastructure-provider economics emerging.
auth.lt:
ExxonMobil is a medium-conviction Visser long built on a single, durable reframe: the constraint on AI's next phase is power, not chips, and the integrated majors are the fastest source of dispatchable electricity – making them 'electron giants,' not oil giants.

The June 2025 new-pick note grounds it in the shape of demand. As AI moves toward embodied/edge deployment – compute in warehouses, hospitals, homes, vehicles – it creates a decentralized, latency-intolerant, 24/7 load that needs local power where solar and nuclear may not be feasible and interconnection lags. Natural gas is the ready, scalable bridge; Exxon's reserves, pipelines and grid-independent modular gas-to-power plants deploy faster than alternatives, and its CFO explicitly frames the company around AI's 'escalating requirements for computing power.' The LNG buildout to 40 mtpa by 2030 extends the same logic internationally.

The January 2026 power-constraint report turns the reframe into a moat. The value doesn't accrue to scale alone but to vertical integration – delivering complete 'molecules to megawatts' systems rather than discrete components, because gigawatt-scale deployments are too complex to assemble multi-vendor. Exxon can dedicate 400-500 MMcf/d to a single campus and sign 20-year bundled contracts; gas turbines, with 100+ week lead times, are the binding choke point. Conservative 10-20 GW math implies $70-100bn of spend, and the arbitrage is the gap between cyclical-commodity pricing and strategic-infrastructure reality.

Honest read: the call has worked directionally (pick.ret ~37%), but Visser tags it only medium conviction, and rightly – the thesis is a re-rating bet that depends on gas remaining the bridge fuel and on AI power demand materializing on the contracted, multi-decade basis he describes. The risks are timing (nuclear/grid catching up faster) and the underlying still being a commodity producer whose multiple the market may be slow to re-rate.

pick.arc: Turbines/transformers sold out through 2028 — 'no transformer, no project'
pick.note: The grid-equipment scarcity call; one of his cleanest power-theme wins.

auth.sb: GE Vernova is Visser's cleanest power-scarcity win: turbines and grid equipment sold out for years, with deliberately capped capacity keeping the bottleneck – and prices – tight. Up ~79%.
auth.mb: The AI bottleneck moved from GPUs to electrons, and GE Vernova sits at the chokepoint. Visser's November 2025 call hinged on a sharp insight: the company is intentionally limiting capacity expansion – growing 'within the same four walls, without pouring concrete' – which keeps the gas-turbine and generator shortage acute and margins fat. CoreWeave cutting capex 40% over undelivered 'power shells,' not weak demand, was his proof that infrastructure, not orders, is the binding constraint. Called near $575, the stock ran to ~$1,029.
auth.lb:
For two years the AI trade was about getting GPUs. Visser's power thesis is that the real shortage has moved downstream to the things that actually energize the data center – gas turbines, generators, transformers – and GE Vernova makes them.

The November 2025 call had a clever twist. GE Vernova isn't racing to add capacity; management said it would grow 'within the same four walls, without pouring concrete or adding new cranes.' Visser read that as deliberate discipline: by not flooding the market with supply, the company keeps its order book sold out for years and its pricing power intact. The bottleneck is the business model.

His evidence that this is real rather than hype came from CoreWeave, which slashed 2025 capex roughly 40% – not because demand softened (backlog surged 85% to $55.6B, demand called 'insatiable') but because a developer couldn't deliver the power infrastructure on time. That's the regime change: orders are abundant, electrons are scarce. From an entry near $575 the stock reached ~$1,029, a ~79% gain and one of his tightest power-theme wins.
auth.st: Grid-equipment scarcity long – gas turbines/generators sold out through ~2028, capacity deliberately capped ('within the same four walls') to keep the bottleneck tight; called 13 Nov 2025 near $575, ran to ~$1,029 (+79%).
auth.mt: The thesis is a supply-discipline scarcity trade. Visser cites CEO Strazik (January 2025): 'significant growth... within the same four walls, without pouring concrete or adding new cranes,' and reads it as intentional capacity restraint – a strategy that 'keeps the bottleneck tight and prices high but does nothing to solve the industry's capacity constraint.' Generators are framed as 'a crucial complement to GE Vernova's gas turbine business,' so investment there prevents 'additional bottlenecks.' The catalyst that validated it (November 2025): CoreWeave cut 2025 capex from $20–23B to $12–14B – ~40% – purely because a third party failed to deliver 'power shells,' even as its revenue backlog surged 85% QoQ to $55.6B and demand was 'insatiable.' Management called it 'a systemic problem that the industry will have to deal with for the foreseeable future.' The mechanism: AI's binding constraint has shifted from GPU availability to grid/power delivery, and GEV controls scarce, slow-to-replicate supply with pricing power protected by its own restraint. +79% from the call.
auth.lt:
GE Vernova is Visser's flagship power-theme winner and arguably his cleanest expression of a scarcity trade. The pick (13 November 2025, near $575, now ~$1,029, +79%) rests on a single durable insight: AI's bottleneck has migrated from chips to the grid, and GEV sits on the supply of gas turbines, generators and grid equipment that is sold out for years.

What makes the thesis sharp is the supply-side discipline. Visser highlights management's choice to grow 'within the same four walls, without pouring concrete' – reading deliberate capacity restraint as the source of durable pricing power. By not solving the industry's shortage, GEV keeps its own bottleneck tight. He extends the logic to generators as a complement that prevents 'additional bottlenecks,' reinforcing the moat.

The corroborating catalyst is unusually concrete. CoreWeave's ~40% 2025 capex cut – driven by undelivered power shells while backlog surged 85% to $55.6B and demand was 'insatiable' – is the cleanest evidence that the constraint is physical infrastructure, not demand. Management's 'systemic problem... for the foreseeable future' line is exactly the kind of multi-year scarcity Visser wants to own. His June 2025 'electron giants' work (Chevron/Exxon as dispatchable power for embodied AI) shows the theme is broad and consistent.

Risks and honest read: the bull case literally depends on supply staying constrained – the threat is the industry (or GEV itself) eventually adding capacity, normalizing turbine lead times and compressing pricing. After a +79% run the stock already prices substantial scarcity, so the durability of order books through 2028 and the pace of competing supply are the swing factors. There is also cyclicality and project-timing risk in heavy electrical equipment. But as a thesis-plus-catalyst package, this is among Visser's tightest, best-evidenced calls – right insight, right timing, clear mechanism, and a real-world stress test that confirmed it.

auth.lb:

The same reports note Walmart and Amazon, which pay billions in interchange fees, have strong incentives to bypass card networks entirely with their own stablecoins, and echo Vinod Khosla's warning that the 2030s may be a graveyard for Fortune 500 firms that can't reinvent fast enough. JPMorgan thus sits on both sides of Visser's lens – a serious adapter, in a structurally contested field.

Bloom's role is implicit – dispatchable, on-site fuel-cell generation for latency-sensitive, grid-constrained AI load – but the notes attach no entry, figure, or return to BE specifically. Conviction is on the power-shortage theme, not the security.
auth.lt:
Bloom Energy carries no Visser pick – two mentions, both inside weekly macro/power pieces, both bullish on the theme but neither establishing a position with an entry or return. The honest framing is that BE is a candidate within a thesis, not a tracked trade.

The thesis is the power bottleneck behind AI. Oracle's September 2025 order shock is the anchor: a $317B QoQ RPO surge (up 359% YoY) toward a ~$500B backlog, driven by inferencing-capacity shortages, with the order book equal to roughly 81% of US quarterly GDP and electricity needs Visser describes as a large share of US demand. The conclusion he draws is to elevate AI 'from a tech story to a macro driver of power, grid, and compute build-outs' and to position for hybrid power – gas, renewables, storage, grid equipment – alongside compute enablers like HBM and networking.

Bloom fits as dispatchable, on-site generation. Its fuel cells address exactly the gap Visser names – power that nuclear and fusion can't supply on the required timeline – for concentrated, latency-sensitive AI load. The August 2025 'long energy, short software' framing reinforces the directional tailwind: AI power demand surging while software margins face agentic disruption.

The honest read and risks: there is no quantified Bloom case here – no entry, no return, no valuation or competitive analysis. The theme is strong and well-evidenced, but BE is one of several ways to play it (gas turbines, grid equipment, storage), and the notes don't differentiate why Bloom over alternatives. Bloom-specific risks – fuel-cell economics, fuel sourcing, project lumpiness, competition from grid-scale gas and from names like GE Vernova that Visser actually picked – go unaddressed. The actionable takeaway is the theme: own dispatchable power exposure into an AI-driven electricity shortage. Bloom is a reasonable but unendorsed expression of it, to be underwritten independently rather than taken as a Visser recommendation.

pick.arc: Legacy-SaaS 'value trap' — moats eroding as agents suppress seat-based demand
pick.note: His signature SHORT / avoid call and the anchor of the 'short software' leg. The stock fell ~37% — the thesis worked.

auth.sb: Visser's signature short: Salesforce is a legacy-SaaS value trap, not a bargain – AI agents are destroying seat-based demand, and the stock fell ~37% as the thesis played out.
auth.mb: Salesforce is the anchor of Visser's 'short software' leg, and the call worked – the stock dropped ~37% from his August 2025 short. His core argument is contrarian to the bulls: investors see Salesforce's drawdown as cheapness, but he sees structural demand destruction. AI agents don't make seat-based software cheaper to want; they erase the need for the seat, the workflow and the standalone interface entirely. By the time an incumbent refactors for today's AI, tomorrow's models have already deleted the workflow.
auth.lb:
Visser turned bearish on Salesforce in August 2025, framing it inside a single trade pair: long energy, short software. Energy demand from AI was surging while software margins faced disruption – Chevron versus Salesforce, he argued, was about to turn decisively in energy's favor. GPT-5's ability to generate software on demand threatened the legacy model directly.

The thesis sharpened into his signature avoid-call. The most common question he heard from investors was 'when do I buy software stocks?' – and he thought they had it exactly backward. Buying Salesforce because it had fallen was the new bubble trade: confusing an abundance-era drawdown for value while scarcity-driven hardware winners ran. Agents, in his telling, are like GLP-1 drugs for software demand – they don't make users switch to a healthier product, they make users consume less overall and exit whole categories.

The stock fell roughly 37%, validating the call. What would change it is a demonstration that agentic AI deepens rather than cannibalizes Salesforce's platform – but Visser's read is that the suppressants are advancing faster than the organizations built to absorb them, which is the opposite of a recovery setup.
auth.st: The mechanism is structural demand destruction, not multiple compression: agentic AI suppresses seat-based SaaS demand the way GLP-1s suppress consumption – users don't substitute, they exit categories. Short worked; stock -~37% from the August 2025 call (~$245 → ~$155).
auth.mt: Visser's bear case rests on a demand-side, not valuation, argument – which is why he calls cheap software the new bubble trade. The analogy is clinical: GLP-1s showed that suppressing the 'noise' of hunger doesn't move users to healthier snacks, it makes them stop snacking – structural demand destruction, not substitution. Agents do the same to legacy SaaS: they erase the standalone workflow, dedicated interface and paid seat. The timing problem compounds it – 'by the time an incumbent refactors for today's AI, tomorrow's models have already erased the need for the workflow entirely.' The variant view that confirms the short: Salesforce openly markets its own AI platform as a way for customers to cut headcount, which Visser reads as the company advertising the suppression of its own seat-based revenue base. The pair trade (Chevron vs Salesforce, long energy / short software) captured both legs.
auth.lt:
Salesforce is the flagship of Visser's software-disruption thesis and his cleanest bearish call. He laid it out on 10 August 2025 amid a blockbuster Q2 earnings season: even with the broad tape strong, the structural story was 'long energy, short software,' with GPT-5's on-demand code generation threatening legacy models and explicitly naming Salesforce, SAP and Oracle as disrupted. He framed Chevron vs Salesforce as the trade turning in energy's favor.

The thesis matured into a refutation of the value crowd. In January 2026 he argued investors had it exactly backward: the parabolic charts of scarcity winners (Micron, SanDisk) versus the drawdowns of Salesforce, Adobe and ServiceNow were being misread as signal for value in what had fallen. His mechanism is demand destruction via 'suppressants' – AI agents that, like GLP-1s, don't shift demand but vanish it, with users exiting entire categories rather than buying a better product.

The Opus 4.5 inflection reinforced it: when code-writing collapses to a commodity and the bottleneck becomes domain knowledge rather than engineering talent, the seat-license model loses its scarcity. The Future-of-Labor report added the structural-incentive layer – companies are racing to replace human workers with digital labor, and Salesforce markets exactly that.

The honest performance read: this one worked, ~37% downside, the rare high-conviction short that delivered. The risk to staying short is symmetrical to the thesis – if Salesforce can prove agentic AI expands its platform value faster than it cannibalizes seats, the demand-destruction story breaks. Visser's standing view is that the suppressants are advancing faster than incumbents can adapt, so he treats rallies as value-trap bait rather than recoveries.

pick.arc: First fully-integrated AI drug-discovery stack; GLP-1 cash funds a biological-AI moat
pick.note: Spotlight 'new pick' reiterated 5 Jun 2026 (the 'GLP-1 Manhattan Project'). Thesis-rich, modest move so far.

auth.sb: Visser's boldest pharma call: Eli Lilly isn't just a GLP-1 winner, it's becoming an AI company – using today's weight-loss cash to build a biological-AI moat rivals can't replicate. He thinks it could be the largest US company by decade's end.
auth.mb: Eli Lilly is Visser's spotlight 'GLP-1 Manhattan Project' pick. The thesis: Lilly is the first fully-integrated AI drug-discovery stack, using enormous GLP-1 cash flow to fund a vertical, domain-specific biological-AI engine (Insilico, Isomorphic Labs, NVIDIA compute) that money alone can't replicate. The real moat is data – decades of proprietary molecules, clinical trials, and failed experiments that you can't scrape from the internet, because in drug discovery, failure is training data. The hot takes are extreme – he believes Lilly could become the largest US company and the most important AI company in the world – with a modest +5% move so far against a thesis-rich, long-horizon setup.
auth.lb:
Visser's Eli Lilly call is one of his most ambitious. It started as a piece on AI transforming pharma's broken economics, where Lilly stood out as the clearest real-world example of a 150-year-old drugmaker rebuilding its entire discovery pipeline around AI – combining Insilico for small-molecule discovery, Isomorphic Labs for AlphaFold-based protein modeling, and NVIDIA compute into an 'AI drug factory.'

By June 2026 it had become a full spotlight thesis – the 'GLP-1 Manhattan Project.' The argument: Lilly is using today's metabolic cash flow to buy tomorrow's biological intelligence. Unlike general AI, where capital, GPUs, and talent are commodities, Lilly's race is vertical and domain-specific – 'closer to building a new operating system for the human body.' The durable moat is data the AI-natives can't get: 'You cannot scrape your way into Lilly's history of molecules,' and crucially, in drug discovery, failure itself is training data.

Where it stands: a high-conviction, long-horizon bet with deliberately extreme framing – Visser thinks Lilly could be the largest US company by the end of the decade and is 'becoming the most important AI company in the world.' The move so far is modest (~+5%), reflecting a thesis that's about compounding optionality, not a single catalyst. The risk is execution and clinical outcomes; the bull case is a compounding GLP-1-cash-to-AI-infrastructure flywheel.
auth.st: First fully-integrated AI drug-discovery stack (Insilico + Isomorphic Labs + NVIDIA compute). GLP-1 cash funds a proprietary biological-AI moat where 'failure is training data.' High conviction, long horizon; +5.4% since the Nov 2025 call.
auth.mt:
Eli Lilly is Visser's healthcare-AI flagship – a high-conviction, thesis-rich long (+5.4% from the Nov 2025 call). The mechanism is a compounding flywheel: GLP-1 revenue generates cash; cash funds AI infrastructure; that infrastructure improves discovery; better discovery expands the pipeline; the pipeline creates more data and revenue; the data improves the models. Lilly is framed as the first fully-integrated AI drug-discovery stack – Insilico for repeatable small-molecule discovery, Isomorphic Labs for AlphaFold-based protein modeling, and NVIDIA powering an 'AI drug factory,' plus a TuneLab/Catalyze360 venture funnel and an NVIDIA co-innovation lab bridging bits to atoms.

The defensible variant is the data moat. Unlike general AI – where capital, GPUs, talent, and public data are commodities – Lilly's edge is vertical and proprietary: 'You cannot scrape your way into Lilly's history of molecules,' and 'in drug discovery, failure is training data.' Visser's deliberately extreme calls: Lilly could be the largest US company by decade-end and 'is becoming the most important AI company in the world,' with peptides 'this decade equivalent to API keys from the prior decade.' The risk is the modest move to date and the long, clinically-gated payoff horizon.
auth.lt:
Eli Lilly is Visser's marquee healthcare-AI thesis – two reports but unusually deep, high conviction, up 5.4% from the 24 November 2025 call. The arc: the first fully-integrated AI drug-discovery stack, with GLP-1 cash funding a biological-AI moat.

The origin is his AI-drug-discovery piece, where Lilly emerged as the clearest real-world case of a 150-year-old pharma rebuilding discovery around AI – Insilico (small-molecule discovery), Isomorphic Labs (AlphaFold-based protein modeling), and NVIDIA compute powering an 'AI drug factory.' Lilly, in his words, 'is no longer experimenting with AI, it is becoming a compute-driven R&D organization.'

The June 2026 spotlight – the 'GLP-1 Manhattan Project' – is the full synthesis. The bull mechanism is a self-reinforcing loop: GLP-1 profits fund AI infrastructure that compresses the biological bottleneck via agentic wet labs, robotics, and digital twins (the NVIDIA co-innovation lab as the bits-to-atoms bridge), turning a pharma company into an AI-infrastructure company 'without investors fully noticing.' M&A and the TuneLab/Catalyze360 network make Lilly an 'aggregator of biological intelligence,' converting external AI innovation into internal pipeline optionality. The deepest part of the moat is data: biology has no open training set, the highest-value data sits inside proprietary trials and failed experiments, and 'in drug discovery, failure is training data' – something AI-natives with models but no closed-loop biology can't replicate.

The honest read: this is a long-horizon, optionality-driven conviction call, not a near-term catalyst trade – the framing is deliberately extreme (largest US company by decade-end; 'the most important AI company in the world'; peptides as the API keys of the decade), and the move so far is modest at +5.4%. The risk is that the payoff is clinically gated and slow, and the thesis depends on the GLP-1 cash engine and the discovery flywheel compounding as drawn. But as a structural ROIC-and-incumbency story, it is among the most fully argued single names in the book.

auth.sb: SanDisk is Visser's evidence that the NAND flash market has flipped to a hard seller's market – buyers now prepay three years of cash just to secure supply. He treats scarcity-driven memory names as the real AI trade, not the cheap fallen software stocks.
auth.mb: SanDisk shows up as a data point in Visser's storage-scarcity argument, not a standalone call. His most telling signal is behavioral: a major NAND producer (SanDisk/Kioxia) now requires three years of prepaid cash to secure supply – terms even TSMC doesn't demand of NVIDIA – evidence of seller's-market discipline in a supply-constrained world. As one supplier put it, “we don't need money, we don't have any product.” He uses SanDisk's parabolic chart, alongside Micron, to argue investors have it backward when they buy beaten-down software – the scarcity-driven hardware winners, not the abundance-era casualties, are where the value sits.
auth.lb:
SanDisk appears in Visser's notes as a marker of how tight the memory market has become. His sharpest observation is behavioral rather than numerical: a major NAND producer – SanDisk/Kioxia – is now requiring three years of prepaid cash to secure supply, terms he points out even TSMC does not demand from NVIDIA. That, he argues, is not normal semiconductor cyclicality but seller's-market discipline in a genuinely supply-constrained environment.

He drives it home with a supplier's blunt line – they don't need money, they have no product. Suppliers, in his read, are short on product, not cash. It is a “flash wall” the AI boom is about to hit.

In his software-versus-hardware debate, SanDisk becomes the foil. Investors look at the parabolic charts of scarcity-driven winners like Micron and SanDisk, compare them to the drawdowns in Salesforce, Adobe and ServiceNow, and conclude the fallen software names are cheap. Visser thinks they have it exactly backward – the scarcity is real and the abundance is the trap. The risk is the usual memory-cycle one: scarcity that severe eventually pulls forward supply, and the cycle turns.
auth.st: Read as the cleanest tell of a NAND seller's market: SanDisk/Kioxia demanding three years of prepaid cash to secure allocation – terms even TSMC doesn't impose on NVIDIA. Visser uses the parabolic Micron/SanDisk charts to argue scarcity-era hardware, not abundance-era software, holds the value.
auth.mt: SanDisk functions as a supply-tightness proxy in Visser's “Flash Wall” thesis. The key signal is behavioral, not numerical: a major NAND producer (SanDisk/Kioxia) now requires three years of prepaid cash to secure supply – terms he stresses even TSMC does not demand of NVIDIA – which he reads as seller's-market discipline, not ordinary semiconductor cyclicality. The corroborating evidence is a supplier's blunt “we don't need money, we don't have any product”: the constraint is product, not capital. He then deploys SanDisk in his contrarian software-versus-hardware call – investors comparing the parabolic charts of scarcity-driven winners (Micron, SanDisk) to the drawdowns in Salesforce, Adobe and ServiceNow infer cheapness in the fallen software, and “I think they have it exactly backward.” The variant view: scarcity is the durable trade, abundance the value trap. The risk is the standard memory cycle – prepayment-level scarcity eventually incentivizes capacity that turns the cycle.
auth.lt:
SanDisk is not a standalone Visser long but a recurring evidence point in two connected arguments about the memory/storage cycle. The first is his “Flash Wall” thesis, where the headline signal is deliberately behavioral: a major NAND producer (SanDisk/Kioxia) is now requiring three years of prepaid cash to secure supply, and he underlines that even TSMC does not impose such extreme terms on NVIDIA. To Visser this is categorically different from normal semiconductor cyclicality – it is seller's-market discipline in a structurally supply-constrained environment, corroborated by a supplier's blunt “We don't need money. We don't have any product.” The bottleneck, in other words, is allocation of physical product, not financing.

The second argument uses SanDisk as the centerpiece of a contrarian call against “cheap” software. Investors, he observes, look at the parabolic charts of scarcity-driven hardware winners like Micron and SanDisk, compare them to the abundance-era drawdowns of Salesforce, Adobe and ServiceNow, and conclude the fallen software is the value. His verdict is unambiguous – “I think they have it exactly backward.” The scarcity in memory is real, demand-anchored and hard to relieve quickly; the cheapness in software reflects AI-driven demand suppression, not opportunity.

The data points are qualitative but pointed: prepayment terms, sold-out language from primary buyers (Phison must raise working capital materially to secure NAND allocation), and the contrast with semiconductor names that price scarcity directly. SanDisk is presented as a clean illustration that the hardware layer is where pricing power currently sits.

The honest read is one of caveat as much as conviction. Visser uses SanDisk thematically rather than modeling it as a single stock, and the obvious risk is intrinsic to memory: scarcity acute enough to force three-year prepayments is exactly the condition that eventually pulls forward capacity and turns the cycle. The trade works while supply stays disciplined and AI-driven demand keeps the market sold out; it unwinds when product finally arrives.

auth.sb: Silver is the only commodity in Visser's 100-name AI portfolio – the cheap, irreplaceable “interface metal” sitting at every electrical failure point in data centers, solar and drones, with supply already in structural deficit. High conviction, up sharply on the year.
auth.mb: Visser's silver call is contrarian by design: most investors lump it in with gold, and he argues that misperception is the opportunity. Silver, he says, is the interface material at the narrow waist of electrical systems – contacts, switches, connectors, solders, conductive pastes – the failure points that decide whether power infrastructure can keep pace with AI. Because it is a tiny share of a data center's bill of materials, demand is price-inelastic: no one cancels a server build because the silver in a connector got pricier. With AI, solar and millions of military drones all pulling on a market already running structural deficits, he calls silver the performance metal of embodied AI – and it is up sharply on the year.
auth.lb:
Silver is Visser's most distinctive commodity idea – the only one in his 100-name compute portfolio – and he opened the thesis in late January 2026. The framing is deliberately against the grain: most investors see silver as just part of the gold trade, and he argues that exact gap between perception and reality is where the edge lives.

His mechanism is physical. Silver is not an energy commodity; it appears where electrons are forced through small spaces under high current and high reliability – contacts, switches, connectors, solders, photovoltaic metallization. These are not headline components, they are failure points, and as electrical systems scale, failures cascade from exactly these interfaces. He draws the analogy to high-bandwidth memory: just as memory became the bottleneck that decided whether GPUs could be used, silver decides whether power infrastructure can keep up with the intelligence it must serve.

The demand side compounds across AI data centers, solar metallization, and a new wave of expendable military drones – Ukraine alone planning millions a year. And it meets a market already running persistent structural deficits, so supply cannot easily respond. The honest read: up sharply on the year (roughly +44% from his anchor), though it gave back about 9% in the month into mid-June. What would change it is substitution at scale or a collapse in the AI/solar/defense demand wave – both of which his “cheap, functionally critical, hard to economize away” argument is built to dismiss.
auth.st: The only commodity in the 100-name compute portfolio: silver as the price-inelastic interface metal at high-current failure points (contacts, connectors, PV metallization, power electronics) into a market already running structural deficits. Demand stacks across AI, solar and expendable drones; up ~44% from the late-Jan anchor, −9% in the month into mid-June.
auth.mt: Visser's silver thesis rests on inelastic byproduct supply meeting non-substitutable interface demand. The mechanism: silver sits at the “narrow waist” of electrical systems – contacts, switches, connectors, solders, conductive pastes, power electronics, PV metallization – the failure points where systems fail discretely rather than degrade smoothly. Because silver is a tiny share of a hyperscale build's bill of materials, demand is price-inelastic: a costlier relay or thermal-interface material does not cancel a server deployment, the system absorbs it. He frames silver as the electrical analog to high-bandwidth memory – the constraint that determines whether power infrastructure can keep pace with compute – and explicitly distinguishes it from copper: “the volume metal of electrification” versus silver as “the performance metal of embodied AI.” Demand stacks across AI data centers, solar, and expendable military drones (Ukraine planning ~4.5m FPV drones in 2025), into a market already running persistent structural deficits. The variant view he keeps stressing is that investors miscategorize it as a precious metal / gold proxy, which is the entry. Performance: up ~44% from the ~$49 anchor toward ~$70, with a ~9% give-back in the month into mid-June.
auth.lt:
Silver is Visser's highest-conviction commodity idea and a genuine outlier – the only commodity in his 100-name compute portfolio – with the thesis opened in late January 2026. The framing is explicitly contrarian: institutional investors keep filing silver under the gold trade, and he argues that misperception is precisely the opportunity, since the metal's real driver is industrial and AI-linked, not monetary.

The mechanism is a physics argument. Silver is not an energy commodity – it appears at the narrow waist of electrical systems where electrons pass through small spaces under high current, high frequency and high reliability: contacts, switches, connectors, solders, conductive pastes, power electronics and photovoltaic metallization. These are failure points, not headline line items, and Visser's key insight is that as electrical systems scale, performance fails discretely – connectors trip, switches arc, data centers go offline – with failures cascading outward from exactly these interfaces. He casts silver as the electrical analog to high-bandwidth memory: the bottleneck that determines whether power infrastructure can keep pace with the intelligence it serves.

The demand stack is what gives the thesis breadth. AI data centers, solar metallization, and a new category – millions of expendable military FPV drones a year (Ukraine alone planning ~4.5 million in 2025) – all pull on the same metal, and crucially the demand is price-inelastic because silver is cheap relative to the value of the hardware it enables. As he puts it, it is economically important precisely because it is cheap, functionally critical, and hard to economize away. Copper stays “the volume metal of electrification,” but silver becomes “the performance metal of embodied AI.” All of this meets a market already running persistent structural deficits, so supply cannot easily flex – silver is largely a byproduct of mining other metals.

The honest read: the trade has worked, up roughly 44% from the ~$49 anchor toward ~$70, though it gave back about 9% in the month into mid-June. The risk symmetry is straightforward – the bear case is large-scale substitution (thrifting silver out of contacts and pastes) or a stall in the AI/solar/defense demand wave; Visser's “cheap, critical, hard to substitute” argument is constructed precisely to rebut the first, while the second is a bet on the whole compute buildout he is already long elsewhere.

auth.sb: Coherent rides Visser's optical-networking chokepoint theme – the bandwidth wall is the next binding constraint on AI, and the Fujikura collapse showed how fragile that physical supply chain is.
auth.mb: Visser's relevant call for Coherent is thematic, through the optical layer: as AI factories scale, copper and insufficient bandwidth become the wall, so optical networking is a finite supply chain everyone is scrambling for at once. He frames the whole AI buildout as resembling the Strait of Hormuz – a narrow choke point a surging global demand is trying to pass through simultaneously – and the Fujikura earnings collapse as the first real glimpse of that fragility in practice.
auth.lb:
Coherent sits inside Visser's optical-networking theme rather than getting a dedicated thesis – the passages reference the connectivity/bandwidth layer Coherent supplies into, not the company by name. Both mentions are framed as risk-aware, not simply bullish.

The December 2025 Nvidia-Groq thread establishes the backdrop: AI's next frontier is decided by who can move data through memory, power and latency constraints most efficiently – an architectural problem where data movement, not transistor count, is the binding cost. That elevates the optical interconnect layer.

By May 2026 the framing turns explicitly to fragility. Visser argues the near-term risk is no longer weak demand but demand so strong the physical world can't keep pace – a synchronized scramble for HBM, advanced packaging, power, cooling and optical networking, "the Strait of Hormuz itself." The Fujikura share collapse despite extraordinary results is his warning shot: the optical supply chain is a chokepoint that can disappoint violently. What changes the call is whether that scarcity resolves to pricing power for optical suppliers or to demand-disappointment shocks.
auth.st: No formal pick; thematic mention via the optical-networking chokepoint. Mechanism: as AI factories scale, copper/bandwidth becomes the wall, making optical interconnect a finite, contested supply chain – framed as a "Strait of Hormuz" choke point; Fujikura's post-earnings collapse cited as the fragility signal.
auth.mt: Coherent is not named directly; the actionable content is the optical-layer thesis it belongs to. The mechanism (December 2025): the next phase of AI "would not be decided simply by who could buy the most transistors" but "It would be decided by who could move data through memory, power, and latency constraints more efficiently." – data movement as the binding constraint, which pushes value toward optical interconnect. The May 2026 escalation reframes the risk: the constraint is now physical supply, with the AI race "a synchronized global scramble for the same finite supply chains: HBM, advanced packaging, power equipment, cooling systems, optical networking, chemicals." The Fujikura (5803) collapse "after earnings despite reporting extraordi"-nary results is the concrete variant-view signal – that even firms inside the chokepoint can sell off hard, making the optical theme a double-edged sword: structurally demanded, but exposed to violent expectations resets.
auth.lt:
Coherent appears in only two reports (December 2025 and May 2026), and notably is referenced through its theme – optical networking and the bandwidth wall – rather than by company-specific analysis. There is no pick, no anchor, no tracked return; the honest framing is that this is theme exposure, not a Visser call on the stock.

The thematic arc is coherent and escalating. December 2025 (the Nvidia-Groq paper) establishes that AI's binding constraint has shifted from transistor density to data movement across memory, power and latency – the architectural case for the optical interconnect layer where Coherent operates. May 2026 ("Bandwidth Wars") sharpens this into a supply-chain-fragility thesis: optical networking is one of the finite chokepoints in a synchronized global scramble, analogized to the Strait of Hormuz.

The risk read is unusually direct because Visser leads with it. His May 2026 thesis is that the biggest near-term danger is demand outrunning the physical supply chain, and the Fujikura collapse – extraordinary results met with a crashing stock – is his evidence that optical names can be punished even when the fundamental demand is real. That cuts directly against a naive long: the bandwidth wall is bullish for demand but means the sub-sector is volatile and expectations-sensitive. He also references a Meta-Corning optical-fiber agreement elsewhere as evidence of how acute the bottleneck is.

Performance read: nothing to grade, because Coherent was never a formal pick. The most honest summary is that Visser is constructive on optical as a structural AI chokepoint but explicitly cautious on the fragility of that supply chain – so Coherent is best understood as a high-beta expression of the bandwidth-wall theme, with the Fujikura episode standing as his own warning that the trade can break violently on disappointment.

auth.lb:
Corning isn't a Visser pick – it shows up as evidence for an argument about where AI bottlenecks are forming next.

That argument is the networking wall. In March 2026 Visser points to Meta's multi-year, up-to-$6B deal to lock up Corning's optical fiber exclusively for its AI data centers. His read: at facility scale, you can't capture the economics of the AI 'factory' if your architecture is choked by the power and latency costs of copper and insufficient bandwidth. By May 2026 the framing sharpens into supply-chain fragility. Visser groups optical networking with HBM, advanced packaging, power and cooling as the finite chokepoints through which a synchronized global AI demand surge is trying to pass at once – his 'Strait of Hormuz' metaphor. auth.mt: Corning is a mention-stance, unsized name used to evidence the optical-networking bottleneck. The March 2026 anchor: Meta's 'multi-year, up to $6 billion agreement with Corning to secure optical fiber exclusively for its AI data centers' demonstrates the 'macro-level networking wall' – 'you cannot realize the economics of the heterogeneous AI factory if your architecture is choked by the power and latency costs of copper wires and insufficient network bandwidth.' By May 2026's 'Bandwidth Wars,' Visser reframes the broader risk: the binding constraint is no longer weak demand but 'demand becoming so strong that the physical world struggles to keep pace,' a 'synchronized global scramble for the same finite supply chains: HBM, advanced packaging, power equipment, cooling systems, optical networking, chemicals.' Corning sits in the optical-networking and chemicals/materials chokepoint. The analytical content is about the chokepoint, not Corning's valuation – no entry, return, or financial figures for GLW appear, so conviction is on the bandwidth-scarcity theme rather than the stock.
auth.lt:
Corning carries no Visser pick – two mentions, both thematic, neither establishing a position with an entry or return. It functions as a concrete data point for the AI networking-and-materials bottleneck rather than a recommendation.

The core evidence is Meta's optical-fiber deal. In March 2026's 'OpenClaw' piece, Visser cites Meta's multi-year, up-to-$6B agreement to secure Corning's optical fiber exclusively for its AI data centers as proof of a facility-scale networking wall: the heterogeneous AI factory can't deliver its economics if copper and insufficient bandwidth choke the architecture. A hyperscaler paying up to $6B for exclusivity is the kind of scarcity signal Visser hunts for – it implies fiber is a binding, slow-to-replicate input.

By May 2026 the thesis generalizes. In 'Bandwidth Wars and the Fragility of the AI Supply Chain,' he argues the near-term risk has flipped from weak demand to demand so strong the physical world can't keep pace, with optical networking listed among the finite chokepoints (alongside HBM, advanced packaging, power, cooling, chemicals) that a synchronized global scramble is straining – his 'Strait of Hormuz' metaphor. Corning, as an optical-fiber and advanced-materials supplier, sits in that passage. Notably, that same paper uses Fujikura's earnings-driven collapse as a warning about how fragile and volatile these supply-chain names can be.

The honest read and risks: there is no quantified Corning case – no entry, no return, no valuation, and no isolation of how much of GLW's business the AI-fiber demand actually moves. The Fujikura cautionary tale Visser himself raises in the same note is the key risk flag: supply-chain-chokepoint stocks can run hard on the scarcity narrative and then gap lower on any execution or earnings disappointment, even with strong underlying demand. For an investor, Corning here is a window into the bandwidth-scarcity theme and a credible beneficiary of it, but an unendorsed, unsized one – worth underwriting on its own fundamentals rather than treating as a tracked Visser call.

auth.lb:

His argument: in the chatbot phase the model was the story, but in the agentic phase the system around the model becomes equally important. If AI is going to act inside a company rather than just respond to a prompt, it needs orchestration – scheduling, state management, file access, data prep, API coordination, security, monitoring, workflow logic – and most of that runs on CPUs.

That shift expands the infrastructure opportunity from GPUs to the full stack, exactly the layer Dell sells. He leans on Intel CFO David Zinsner's line that 'the CPU has become cool again,' with the agentic-AI orchestration pickup beginning in the second half of 2025. AI-server revenue up 757% year over year is the headline proof of demand.

The thesis has worked big from the March thematic call (pick.ret ~211%). The honest wrinkle is timing: the explicit spotlight 'new pick' landed 1 June 2026 near the all-time high around $466, and the stock has since come off – the structural call is strong, but the entry point on the spotlight version was unforgiving.

auth.lt:
Dell is one of Visser's stronger AI-infrastructure longs, built on a single sharp insight: the agentic transition expands the trade from GPUs to the whole rack, and Dell owns the full-stack integration.

The thesis, laid out 16 March 2026, distinguishes the phases. In the chatbot phase the model is the focal point; in the agentic phase the system around the model – orchestration: scheduling, state management, file access, data prep, API coordination, security, monitoring, workflow logic – becomes equally important, and most of it runs on CPUs. Visser leans on Intel CFO David Zinsner's framing that demand picked up in H2 2025 and 'the CPU has become cool again,' because orchestration away from just running the LLM has to run on CPUs. The risk is the inverse of the thesis's strength: it's a system-integration/server business with thinner economics than the silicon layer, and the post-ATH pullback shows the trade can get crowded and overextended. The call to make is between conviction in the full-rack/CPU-orchestration structural story – which Visser rates high – and discipline on entry, since the 1 June spotlight timing is the cautionary note baked into the pick itself.
